flashcards.front: What is the PROBATE ESTATE vs GROSS ESTATE?
flashcards.back:
PROBATE ESTATE:
• Assets passing through will
• Subject to probate process
• Court supervised

GROSS ESTATE (Federal):
• All assets at death for estate tax
• Includes non-probate assets
• Life insurance, joint property, IRAs
• Generally larger than probate

Planning goal: Minimize both
flashcards.example: 2024 exemption: $13.61M per person. Estate of $15M: taxable amount $1.39M × 40% = $556,000 federal estate tax.

flashcards.front: What are PROBATE AVOIDANCE techniques?
flashcards.back:
Probate Avoidance:

• BENEFICIARY DESIGNATIONS: IRAs, life insurance
• JOINT TENANCY: WROS passes automatically
• POD/TOD ACCOUNTS: Payable/Transfer on Death
• REVOCABLE LIVING TRUST: Assets titled in trust
• COMMUNITY PROPERTY: Spouse succession
• SMALL ESTATE PROCEDURES: Below threshold

Note: Avoids probate, not estate tax
flashcards.example: 2024 exemption: $13.61M per person. Estate of $15M: taxable amount $1.39M × 40% = $556,000 federal estate tax.

flashcards.front: What are the TYPES of wills?
flashcards.back:
Types of Wills:

• SIMPLE: Basic distribution
• TESTAMENTARY TRUST: Creates trust at death
• POUR-OVER: "Pours" assets into living trust
• HOLOGRAPHIC: Handwritten, no witnesses
• NUNCUPATIVE: Oral (limited recognition)
• MUTUAL/RECIPROCAL: Coordinated between spouses

Most states require witnesses (usually 2)
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What are will CONTEST grounds?
flashcards.back: Will Contest Grounds:\n\n• LACK OF CAPACITY: Didn

flashcards.front: What are the PARTIES to a trust?
flashcards.back:
Trust Parties:

• GRANTOR (Settlor/Trustor): Creates trust
• TRUSTEE: Manages trust assets
• BENEFICIARY: Receives benefits
  - Income beneficiary
  - Remainder beneficiary

Roles can overlap:
• Grantor can be trustee
• Grantor can be beneficiary
• Same person can hold multiple roles
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: Compare REVOCABLE vs IRREVOCABLE trusts
flashcards.back:
REVOCABLE TRUST:
• Grantor can modify/terminate
• No gift tax on funding
• No asset protection
• Included in estate
• Avoids probate

IRREVOCABLE TRUST:
• Cannot change without beneficiary consent
• Gift on funding (if complete)
• Asset protection possible
• Removed from estate
• More tax benefits
flashcards.example: Annual exclusion $18,000 (2024). Parent gifts $50,000: $32,000 uses lifetime exemption or triggers gift tax.

flashcards.front: What is a LIVING (Inter Vivos) trust?
flashcards.back: Living Trust:\n• Created during grantor
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What is a TESTAMENTARY trust?
flashcards.back:
Testamentary Trust:
• Created by will at death
• Irrevocable once activated
• Subject to probate first

Common Uses:
• Minor children
• Spendthrift protection
• Marital trusts
• Bypass/credit shelter

Advantage: Court supervision
Disadvantage: No incapacity protection
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What is a CREDIT SHELTER (Bypass) trust?
flashcards.back: Credit Shelter Trust (CST):\n• Captures deceased spouse
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What is a QTIP trust?
flashcards.back:
QTIP (Qualified Terminable Interest Property):
• Qualifies for marital deduction
• Yet controls ultimate distribution

Requirements:
• Income to spouse for life
• Income at least annually
• No power to appoint to others
• Executor makes QTIP election

Benefits:
• Second marriage planning
• Control remainder beneficiaries
• Defers estate tax
flashcards.example: 2024 exemption: $13.61M per person. Estate of $15M: taxable amount $1.39M × 40% = $556,000 federal estate tax.

flashcards.front: What is a CHARITABLE REMAINDER TRUST (CRT)?
flashcards.back:
Charitable Remainder Trust:
• Irrevocable trust
• Income to donor/beneficiary, remainder to charity

Types:
• CRAT: Fixed annuity (5-50%)
• CRUT: Percentage of value annually

Benefits:
• Income tax deduction (PV of remainder)
• No capital gains on appreciated asset
• Income stream
• Estate tax savings

flashcards.front: What is a CHARITABLE LEAD TRUST (CLT)?
flashcards.back:
Charitable Lead Trust:
• Opposite of CRT
• Income to charity first, remainder to family

Types:
• CLAT: Fixed annuity to charity
• CLUT: Percentage of value to charity

Benefits:
• Reduces gift/estate tax on transfer
• Assets grow for heirs
• Charitable income stream

Best when: Interest rates low, assets appreciate

flashcards.front: What is a SPENDTHRIFT trust?
flashcards.back: Spendthrift Trust:\n• Restricts beneficiary access\n• Protects from beneficiary
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What is a SPECIAL NEEDS trust?
flashcards.back:
Special Needs Trust (SNT):
• For disabled beneficiaries
• Preserves government benefits
• SSI, Medicaid eligibility

Types:
• FIRST PARTY: Funded with beneficiary
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What is a GRANTOR RETAINED ANNUITY TRUST (GRAT)?
flashcards.back:
GRAT:
• Grantor transfers assets
• Receives annuity for term
• Remainder to beneficiaries

Tax Benefits:
• Gift = FMV - PV of annuity
• Can be "zeroed out" (minimal gift)
• Excess growth passes tax-free

Risk:
• Grantor must survive term
• IRS 7520 rate affects value
• No step-up in basis

flashcards.front: What is a QUALIFIED PERSONAL RESIDENCE TRUST (QPRT)?
flashcards.back:
QPRT:
• Transfer home to irrevocable trust
• Retain right to live there for term
• Home passes to beneficiaries at end

Benefits:
• Discounted gift value
• Removes future appreciation
• Can rent at FMV after term

Risks:
• Must survive term
• Lose home if term expires
• No step-up in basis
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What is the ESTATE TAX exemption and rate?
flashcards.back:
Federal Estate Tax (2026):

• EXEMPTION: $7.0 million per person (TCJA sunset)
• MFJ: $14.0 million (with portability)
• RATE: 40% flat on taxable estate

Note: Pre-2026 gifts protected by anti-clawback rule

Taxable Estate = Gross Estate - Deductions
(Marital, charitable, expenses, debts)
flashcards.example: 2024 exemption: $13.61M per person. Estate of $15M: taxable amount $1.39M × 40% = $556,000 federal estate tax.

flashcards.front: What is PORTABILITY?
flashcards.back:
Portability:
• Transfer unused estate tax exemption to surviving spouse
• DSUE: Deceased Spousal Unused Exclusion

Requirements:
• File estate tax return (706) at first death
• Elect portability on return
• Even if no tax due

Benefits:
• Simpler than bypass trust
• Flexibility

Limitations:
• No growth protection
• Only one spouse (not previous)
flashcards.example: 2024 exemption: $13.61M per person. Estate of $15M: taxable amount $1.39M × 40% = $556,000 federal estate tax.

flashcards.front: What are ESTATE TAX deductions?
flashcards.back:
Estate Tax Deductions:

• MARITAL DEDUCTION: Unlimited to spouse
• CHARITABLE DEDUCTION: Unlimited to charity
• DEBTS & MORTGAGES: Outstanding at death
• FUNERAL EXPENSES: Reasonable costs
• ADMINISTRATION: Executor fees, legal, accounting
• LOSSES: Casualty/theft during administration
• STATE DEATH TAXES: Credit or deduction

Gross Estate - Deductions = Taxable Estate
flashcards.example: 2024 exemption: $13.61M per person. Estate of $15M: taxable amount $1.39M × 40% = $556,000 federal estate tax.

flashcards.front: What is the GIFT TAX framework?
flashcards.back:
Gift Tax Framework:

ANNUAL EXCLUSION: $19,000/donee (2026)
LIFETIME EXEMPTION: $7.0M (TCJA sunset, shared with estate)
RATE: 40%

Structure:
• Gift over annual exclusion uses lifetime
• Gift tax return if over annual (706)
• Actually pay tax only if exceed lifetime

Gift tax paid by DONOR, not donee
flashcards.example: Annual exclusion $18,000 (2024). Parent gifts $50,000: $32,000 uses lifetime exemption or triggers gift tax.

flashcards.front: What are GIFT TAX exclusions (not counted)?
flashcards.back:
Not Taxable Gifts:

• ANNUAL EXCLUSION: $19,000/person (2026)
• TUITION: Paid directly to institution
• MEDICAL: Paid directly to provider
• POLITICAL: Campaign contributions
• SPOUSAL: Unlimited to US citizen spouse
• CHARITABLE: To qualified organizations

These don
flashcards.example: Annual exclusion $18,000 (2024). Parent gifts $50,000: $32,000 uses lifetime exemption or triggers gift tax.

flashcards.front: What is GIFT SPLITTING?
flashcards.back: Gift Splitting:\n• Married couples split gifts\n• One spouse

flashcards.front: What makes a gift COMPLETE for tax purposes?
flashcards.back:
Complete Gift Requirements:
• DELIVERY: Property transferred
• DONATIVE INTENT: Meant as gift
• DOMINION & CONTROL: Donor gives up control
• ACCEPTANCE: Donee accepts

Incomplete if:
• Donor retains control
• Revocable transfer
• Power to change beneficiary

Incomplete gift: No tax, still in estate

flashcards.front: What DISCOUNTS apply to estate/gift valuations?
flashcards.back:
Valuation Discounts:

LACK OF MARKETABILITY:
• Not publicly traded
• 15-35% typical

LACK OF CONTROL (Minority):
• Non-controlling interest
• 15-40% typical

Combined: 25-50% possible

Apply to:
• FLP interests
• Closely held business
• Real estate partnerships

IRS scrutinizes; need appraisal

flashcards.front: What is ALTERNATE VALUATION DATE?
flashcards.back:
Alternate Valuation Date:
• Value estate 6 months after death
• Instead of date of death

Requirements:
• Must reduce gross estate AND estate tax
• Election is all or nothing
• Cannot cherry-pick assets
• Assets sold before = sale date value

Use when: Market declines after death
File election on estate return
flashcards.example: 2024 exemption: $13.61M per person. Estate of $15M: taxable amount $1.39M × 40% = $556,000 federal estate tax.

flashcards.front: What is a DURABLE POWER OF ATTORNEY?
flashcards.back: Durable Power of Attorney:\n• Agent acts on principal

flashcards.front: What is a HEALTHCARE POWER OF ATTORNEY?
flashcards.back:
Healthcare Power of Attorney (Healthcare Proxy):
• Agent makes medical decisions
• When principal cannot

Decisions include:
• Treatment choices
• Hospitalization
• Surgery
• Medications
• End-of-life care

Often combined with living will
HIPAA authorization important

flashcards.front: What is a LIVING WILL?
flashcards.back:
Living Will:
• Expresses end-of-life wishes
• When terminally ill/permanently unconscious

Addresses:
• Life support
• Feeding tubes
• Resuscitation
• Pain management
• Organ donation

Not same as healthcare POA:
• Living will = Instructions
• Healthcare POA = Decision maker

Best to have both

flashcards.front: What is a DNR order?
flashcards.back:
DNR (Do Not Resuscitate):
• Medical order not to perform CPR
• Must be signed by physician
• Different from living will

Types:
• In-hospital DNR
• Out-of-hospital DNR
• POLST (Physician Orders for Life-Sustaining Treatment)

Consistent with living will wishes
Can be revoked at any time

flashcards.front: What are forms of PROPERTY OWNERSHIP?
flashcards.back:
Property Ownership Types:

• TENANCY IN COMMON: No survivorship, each owns %
• JOINT TENANCY (WROS): Equal shares, survivorship
• TENANCY BY ENTIRETY: Married couples, survivorship
• COMMUNITY PROPERTY: 50-50, some states
• SOLE OWNERSHIP: One owner

Each has different probate/estate treatment

flashcards.front: How is JOINT TENANCY with right of survivorship treated?
flashcards.back:
JTWROS Treatment:

PROBATE:
• Avoids probate (passes by operation of law)

ESTATE TAX:
• Spouses: 50% in each estate
• Non-spouses: Amount contributed in first decedent

INCOME TAX:
• Survivor gets stepped-up basis on decedent
flashcards.example: 2024 exemption: $13.61M per person. Estate of $15M: taxable amount $1.39M × 40% = $556,000 federal estate tax.

flashcards.front: What are COMMUNITY PROPERTY states?
flashcards.back:
Community Property States (9+):
• Arizona
• California
• Idaho
• Louisiana
• Nevada
• New Mexico
• Texas
• Washington
• Wisconsin

Alaska: Opt-in

Benefit: Double step-up in basis at first death
Separate property: Owned before marriage, gifts, inheritance

flashcards.front: What assets pass by BENEFICIARY DESIGNATION?
flashcards.back:
Beneficiary Designation Assets:
• Life insurance policies
• IRAs and 401(k)s
• Annuities
• POD bank accounts
• TOD brokerage accounts
• HSAs
• Pension plans

SuperPriority:
• Override will provisions
• Must keep updated
• Review after life events
flashcards.example: Term: $1M coverage, 20-year term, $50/month. Whole life: $500,000 coverage, $400/month, builds cash value.

flashcards.front: What is PER STIRPES vs PER CAPITA?
flashcards.back:
Distribution Methods:

PER STIRPES (By Representation):
• Deceased beneficiary

flashcards.front: What is a FAMILY LIMITED PARTNERSHIP (FLP)?
flashcards.back:
Family Limited Partnership:
• Family members as partners
• Parents = General partners (control)
• Children = Limited partners (economic)

Benefits:
• Valuation discounts (30-40%)
• Centralized management
• Asset protection for LPs
• Gradual wealth transfer

IRS scrutiny: Must have legitimate purpose
Avoid deathbed transfers

flashcards.front: What is the GENERATION-SKIPPING TRANSFER TAX (GSTT)?
flashcards.back:
GSTT:
• Tax on transfers to "skip persons"
• 2+ generations below transferor
• Prevents skipping estate tax

2026 Exemption: $7.0M (TCJA sunset, same as estate)
Rate: 40% (flat)

Transfer Types:
• DIRECT SKIP: To skip person
• TAXABLE TERMINATION: Trust interest ends
• TAXABLE DISTRIBUTION: From trust to skip person
flashcards.example: 2024 exemption: $13.61M per person. Estate of $15M: taxable amount $1.39M × 40% = $556,000 federal estate tax.

flashcards.front: What is a DYNASTY TRUST?
flashcards.back:
Dynasty Trust:
• Multigenerational trust
• Designed to last perpetually
• Avoids estate tax each generation

Features:
• Irrevocable
• Uses GSTT exemption
• State law dependent (rule against perpetuities)
• DE, SD, NV, others allow

Can grow to billions tax-free
Great use of GSTT exemption
flashcards.example: 2024 exemption: $13.61M per person. Estate of $15M: taxable amount $1.39M × 40% = $556,000 federal estate tax.

flashcards.front: What is a DONOR ADVISED FUND (DAF)?
flashcards.back:
Donor Advised Fund:
• Account at public charity
• Irrevocable contribution
• Immediate deduction
• Recommend grants over time

Benefits:
• Simple, low cost
• Can contribute appreciated assets
• Anonymous giving option
• No minimum distribution

Limitation: Advisory capacity only
Not technically a trust
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What is a PRIVATE FOUNDATION?
flashcards.back:
Private Foundation:
• Family-controlled charity
• 501(c)(3) status
• Investment income excise tax (1.39%)

Requirements:
• 5% minimum distribution annually
• No self-dealing
• Limit on business holdings

Vs. DAF:
• More control, more expense
• Family employment allowed
• Lower deduction limits
• Administrative burden

flashcards.front: What is a CHARITABLE GIFT ANNUITY?
flashcards.back:
Charitable Gift Annuity:
• Contract with charity
• Gift exchange for fixed payments
• Partially tax-free payments

Benefits:
• Immediate partial deduction
• Lifetime income stream
• Capital gains spread if appreciated asset

Risk:
• Unsecured obligation of charity
• Fixed payments (no inflation adjustment)
• Irrevocable

flashcards.front: How is LIFE INSURANCE treated in estate?
flashcards.back:
Life Insurance Estate Treatment:

INCOME TAX:
• Death benefit generally income tax-free

ESTATE TAX:
• Included if owner OR incidents of ownership
• 3-year lookback for transfers

Planning:
• ILIT owns policy = Out of estate
• Another person owns = Out of estate
• Charity owns = Deduction

Beneficiary ≠ estate owner
flashcards.example: 2024 exemption: $13.61M per person. Estate of $15M: taxable amount $1.39M × 40% = $556,000 federal estate tax.

flashcards.front: What are BUSINESS SUCCESSION planning options?
flashcards.back:
Business Succession Options:

• OUTRIGHT SALE: Third party
• FAMILY TRANSFER: Gift, sale, GRAT
• BUY-SELL AGREEMENT: Co-owners or entity
• ESOP: Employee stock ownership plan
• MANAGEMENT BUYOUT: Employees purchase
• IPO: Public offering

Each has different tax and control implications
Start planning early

flashcards.front: What is SECTION 303 STOCK REDEMPTION?
flashcards.back:
Section 303:
• Stock redemption to pay estate taxes
• Treated as capital gain (not dividend)

Requirements:
• Stock >35% of gross estate
• Used for death taxes, funeral, admin
• Redemption within time limits

Benefit:
• Capital gains treatment
• Stepped-up basis = Less/no gain
• Provides estate liquidity
flashcards.example: 2024 exemption: $13.61M per person. Estate of $15M: taxable amount $1.39M × 40% = $556,000 federal estate tax.

flashcards.front: What is SECTION 6166 installment payment?
flashcards.back:
Section 6166:
• Defer estate tax on closely held business
• Installment payments over 14 years

Requirements:
• Business >35% of adjusted gross estate
• Interest only for first 4 years
• Principal over next 10 years

Interest Rate:
• 2% on first $1M+ equivalent
• Higher rate on excess

Benefit: Liquidity relief
flashcards.example: 2024 exemption: $13.61M per person. Estate of $15M: taxable amount $1.39M × 40% = $556,000 federal estate tax.

flashcards.front: What are FIDUCIARY DUTIES of trustees/executors?
flashcards.back: Fiduciary Duties:\n\n• LOYALTY: Act in beneficiary
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What is the SECURE Act impact on inherited IRAs?
flashcards.back:
SECURE Act Inherited IRAs:

PRE-2020:
• Stretch over beneficiary
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: Should you name a TRUST as IRA beneficiary?
flashcards.back:
Trust as IRA Beneficiary:

PROS:
• Control distributions
• Protect beneficiary (spendthrift)
• Special needs planning
• Minor children

CONS:
• Complex rules (conduit vs accumulation)
• Compressed trust tax brackets
• Accumulation: Oldest beneficiary rule
• Higher taxes if accumulated

Best: "See-through" conduit trust
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: Which states have ESTATE or INHERITANCE taxes?
flashcards.back:
State Death Taxes:

ESTATE TAX (tax on estate):
• CT, DC, HI, IL, ME, MD, MA, MN, NY, OR, RI, VT, WA
• Lower exemptions than federal

INHERITANCE TAX (tax on recipient):
• IA, KY, MD, NE, NJ, PA
• Rates vary by relationship

MD has both
Some states decoupled from federal
flashcards.example: 2024 exemption: $13.61M per person. Estate of $15M: taxable amount $1.39M × 40% = $556,000 federal estate tax.

flashcards.front: What documents are in a complete ESTATE PLAN?
flashcards.back:
Core Estate Planning Documents:

1. WILL: Property distribution
2. REVOCABLE TRUST: Probate avoidance
3. DURABLE POA: Financial decisions
4. HEALTHCARE POA: Medical decisions
5. LIVING WILL: End-of-life wishes
6. HIPAA AUTHORIZATION: Medical access

Also consider:
• Beneficiary designations
• Letter of instruction
• Digital asset instructions
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: When should ESTATE PLANS be reviewed?
flashcards.back:
Estate Plan Review Triggers:

LIFE EVENTS:
• Marriage/divorce
• Birth/adoption
• Death of beneficiary/fiduciary
• Significant wealth change
• Move to different state
• Health changes

LEGAL CHANGES:
• Tax law changes
• State law changes

MINIMUM: Every 3-5 years
After TCJA sunset: 2026 review critical
flashcards.example: CFP professional managing $500,000 must recommend lowest-cost index funds if appropriate, not high-commission products.

flashcards.front: What is a PREMARITAL (Prenuptial) AGREEMENT?
flashcards.back:
Premarital Agreement:
• Contract before marriage
• Defines property rights

Addresses:
• Separate vs marital property
• Spousal support (limits)
• Death/divorce distribution
• Debt liability

Enforcement Requirements:
• Written
• Full disclosure
• Voluntary
• Fair at execution
• Independent counsel recommended

flashcards.front: What is ELECTIVE SHARE?
flashcards.back:
Elective Share (Forced Share):
• Spouse can elect against will
• Receive statutory minimum
• Prevents disinheritance

Typical: 1/3 to 1/2 of estate
Varies by state

UMA (Uniform Probate Code):
• Increasing percentage by years married
• Reaches 50% after 15 years

Prenup can waive elective share

flashcards.front: What are ASSET PROTECTION strategies?
flashcards.back:
Asset Protection Tools:

• EXEMPTIONS: Homestead, retirement
• TITLING: Tenancy by entirety
• INSURANCE: Umbrella, liability
• BUSINESS ENTITIES: LLC, LP
• IRREVOCABLE TRUSTS: Domestic, offshore
• QUALIFIED PLANS: ERISA protection

Rules:
• Plan before liability arises
• Fraudulent transfer concerns
• Balance with other goals
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What is a DOMESTIC ASSET PROTECTION TRUST (DAPT)?
flashcards.back:
DAPT (Self-Settled Trust):
• Irrevocable trust
• Grantor is beneficiary
• Creditor protection

Requirements:
• DAPT state: NV, DE, SD, AK, etc.
• Resident trustee
• Wait period: 2-4 years

Limitations:
• Fraudulent transfer rules
• Unknown if respected in non-DAPT states
• Federal creditors (IRS)
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What are trust INCOME TAX brackets?
flashcards.back:
Trust Tax Brackets 2024:
• 10%: $0 - $3,100
• 24%: $3,100 - $11,150
• 35%: $11,150 - $15,200
• 37%: Over $15,200

Vs. Individuals: Much more compressed
37% at $15K trust vs $578K+ single

Plan to distribute income (DNI)
Grantor trust: Taxed to grantor
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What is DISTRIBUTABLE NET INCOME (DNI)?
flashcards.back:
DNI:
• Limits deduction for trust distributions
• Flows character of income to beneficiary

Concept:
• Trust distributes = Beneficiary taxed
• Trust retains = Trust taxed
• Can
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What is a GRANTOR TRUST?
flashcards.back:
Grantor Trust:
• Trust where grantor pays income tax
• "Defective" for income tax
• But removed from estate for estate tax

Triggering Powers:
• Revocable
• Benefit grantor/spouse
• Borrow without security
• Substitute assets

IDGT (Intentionally Defective):
• Estate planning tool
• Grantor pays tax = More growth for beneficiaries
flashcards.example: 2024 exemption: $13.61M per person. Estate of $15M: taxable amount $1.39M × 40% = $556,000 federal estate tax.

flashcards.front: What is a CRUMMEY TRUST?
flashcards.back:
Crummey Trust:
• Gifts qualify for annual exclusion
• Even though trust is irrevocable

How it works:
• Beneficiary gets withdrawal right
• Usually 30-60 days
• "Present interest" requirement met
• Typically lapses

Crummey Notice:
• Must notify beneficiaries
• Document withdrawal right
• Common with ILIT
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: How should DIGITAL ASSETS be planned for?
flashcards.back:
Digital Asset Planning:

Types:
• Financial (crypto, online accounts)
• Personal (photos, social media)
• Business (websites, domains)

Planning Steps:
• Inventory digital assets
• Store passwords securely
• Grant fiduciary access (state law allowing)
• Include in estate documents
• Consider hardware wallet access

RUSA: Revised Uniform Fiduciary Access to Digital Assets Act
flashcards.example: CFP professional managing $500,000 must recommend lowest-cost index funds if appropriate, not high-commission products.

flashcards.front: What is a LETTER OF INSTRUCTION?
flashcards.back:
Letter of Instruction:
• Informal document (not legally binding)
• Supplements will and trust

Contents:
• Funeral wishes
• Asset locations
• Passwords / digital access
• Personal messages
• Pet care instructions
• Important contacts

Advantage: Easily updated
Keep accessible (not in safe deposit)
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: Grantor Retained Annuity Trust vs Unitrust
flashcards.back:
GRAT (Grantor Retained Annuity Trust):
• Fixed annual payment
• Best when assets expected to appreciate
• "Zeroed-out" GRAT = no gift tax

GRUT (Grantor Retained Unitrust):
• Percentage of annual trust value
• Payment fluctuates with value
• Less common than GRAT

Both: Grantor must outlive term

flashcards.front: Gift Tax Annual Exclusion
flashcards.back:
Annual exclusion (2026): $19,000

• Per donee, per year
• Unlimited number of recipients
• Gift splitting with spouse = $38,000
• No return needed if under limit

Must be present interest gift
(Crummey power for trusts)
flashcards.example: Annual exclusion $18,000 (2024). Parent gifts $50,000: $32,000 uses lifetime exemption or triggers gift tax.

flashcards.front: Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust
flashcards.back:
ILIT purposes:
• Remove life insurance from estate
• Provide liquidity for estate taxes
• Control distribution to beneficiaries

Key rules:
• 3-year look-back for transfers
• Crummey notices for gift tax exclusion
• Trustee pays premiums
• Insured cannot be trustee
flashcards.example: 2024 exemption: $13.61M per person. Estate of $15M: taxable amount $1.39M × 40% = $556,000 federal estate tax.

flashcards.front: Payable on Death vs Transfer on Death
flashcards.back:
POD (Payable on Death):
• Bank accounts, CDs
• Beneficiary receives at death

TOD (Transfer on Death):
• Brokerage accounts, securities
• Beneficiary receives at death

Both:
• Avoid probate
• Revocable during lifetime
• Override will provisions
• Simple beneficiary designation

flashcards.front: What is the formula for PRESENT VALUE of a lump sum?
flashcards.back:
PV = FV / (1 + r)^n

Where:
• FV = Future Value
• r = Interest rate per period
• n = Number of periods
flashcards.example: To have $50,000 in 10 years at 5% return: PV = $50,000 / (1.05)^10 = $30,696 needed today.

flashcards.front: What is the formula for FUTURE VALUE of a lump sum?
flashcards.back:
FV = PV × (1 + r)^n

Where:
• PV = Present Value
• r = Interest rate per period
• n = Number of periods
flashcards.example: To have $50,000 in 10 years at 5% return: PV = $50,000 / (1.05)^10 = $30,696 needed today.

flashcards.front: What is NET WORTH and how is it calculated?
flashcards.back:
Net Worth = Total Assets - Total Liabilities

Also called:
• Owner

flashcards.front: What is the formula for SAVINGS RATE?
flashcards.back:
Savings Rate = Annual Savings ÷ Gross Income

Recommended: 10-15% minimum

Includes: 401(k), IRA, taxable savings
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: How do you calculate REAL RATE OF RETURN?
flashcards.back:
Approximate: Real Rate ≈ Nominal Rate - Inflation

Exact: (1 + Nominal) ÷ (1 + Inflation) - 1

Example: 8% nominal, 3% inflation
Exact: (1.08/1.03) - 1 = 4.85%

flashcards.front: What are the Federal Reserve"s PRIMARY TOOLS?
flashcards.back:
1. OPEN MARKET OPERATIONS (most used)
   - Buy bonds = increase money supply
   - Sell bonds = decrease money supply

2. DISCOUNT RATE
   - Rate charged to banks

3. RESERVE REQUIREMENTS
   - % banks must hold

flashcards.front: What are LEADING, LAGGING, and COINCIDENT indicators?
flashcards.back:
LEADING (predict future):
• Building permits, stock prices, money supply

COINCIDENT (current state):
• GDP, employment, industrial production

LAGGING (confirm trends):
• Unemployment rate, CPI, prime rate

flashcards.front: What is the FIDUCIARY standard for CFP professionals?
flashcards.back: CFP® professionals must:\n• Act in client
flashcards.example: CFP professional managing $500,000 must recommend lowest-cost index funds if appropriate, not high-commission products.

flashcards.front: What are the MATERIAL CONFLICTS of interest CFPs must disclose?
flashcards.back:
Must disclose:
• Compensation arrangements
• Referral fees
• Incentives from third parties
• Ownership interests
• Relationships with entities
• Any bias in recommendations

flashcards.front: What is a COVERDELL ESA and its key features?
flashcards.back:
Coverdell Education Savings Account:
• $2,000/year contribution limit
• Income limits apply
• Tax-free qualified withdrawals
• K-12 and higher education expenses
• Must use by age 30
• Beneficiary can be changed

flashcards.front: Compare 529 plan vs Coverdell ESA
flashcards.back:
529 PLAN:
• Higher/no limits
• Higher ed + K-12 tuition
• No income limits
• Age 30 for Roth rollover

COVERDELL:
• $2,000/year limit
• K-12 all expenses
• Income limits
• Use by age 30

flashcards.front: What is the AMERICAN OPPORTUNITY TAX CREDIT (AOTC)?
flashcards.back:
AOTC Features:
• Up to $2,500/student
• 100% of first $2,000 + 25% of next $2,000
• First 4 years of higher ed only
• 40% refundable ($1,000 max)
• Income phaseouts apply
• No felony drug conviction

flashcards.front: What is the LIFETIME LEARNING CREDIT?
flashcards.back:
LLC Features:
• Up to $2,000/return
• 20% of first $10,000
• No year limit
• NOT refundable
• Any post-secondary education
• Skills development courses OK
• Income phaseouts apply

flashcards.front: What are the components of ACTIVE LISTENING?
flashcards.back:
• Face client, maintain eye contact
• Avoid interrupting
• Ask clarifying questions
• Paraphrase to confirm understanding
• Notice nonverbal cues
• Summarize key points
• Show empathy

flashcards.front: What is MENTAL ACCOUNTING?
flashcards.back:
Mental Accounting: Treating money differently based on source or intended use.

Examples:
• Tax refund = "fun money"
• Inheritance = special investments
• Separate buckets for different goals

Can lead to suboptimal decisions

flashcards.front: What is CONFIRMATION BIAS?
flashcards.back:
Confirmation Bias: Seeking information that confirms existing beliefs while ignoring contradicting evidence.

In investing:
• Only reading bullish news on owned stocks
• Dismissing negative analyst reports
• Overconfidence in decisions

flashcards.front: What is LOSS AVERSION?
flashcards.back:
Loss Aversion: Losses hurt about 2x more than equivalent gains feel good.

Consequences:
• Holding losers too long
• Selling winners too early
• Avoiding beneficial risks
• Reference point dependence

flashcards.front: What is ANCHORING bias?
flashcards.back:
Anchoring: Over-relying on initial information when making decisions.

Examples:
• Fixating on purchase price
• Initial offer in negotiations
• First number heard sets expectations

Combat: Use multiple reference points

flashcards.front: What is RECENCY BIAS?
flashcards.back:
Recency Bias: Overweighting recent events vs long-term trends.

Examples:
• Chasing recent performance
• Expecting markets to continue current trend
• Forgetting historical cycles

Combat: Focus on long-term data

flashcards.front: What is the DEBT-TO-ASSET ratio?
flashcards.back:
Debt-to-Asset = Total Debt ÷ Total Assets

Interpretation:
• Lower is better
• <50% = conservative
• >80% = concerning leverage

Shows portion of assets funded by debt

flashcards.front: What is the CURRENT RATIO and what does it measure?
flashcards.back:
Current Ratio = Current Assets ÷ Current Liabilities

Measures: Ability to pay short-term obligations

• >1.0 = Can cover current debts
• <1.0 = Liquidity concern
• Too high = inefficient use of assets

flashcards.front: What is the BUSINESS CYCLE and its phases?
flashcards.back:
Business Cycle Phases:

1. EXPANSION - GDP growing
2. PEAK - Max growth
3. CONTRACTION/RECESSION - Declining
4. TROUGH - Bottom

Recession = 2+ quarters negative GDP

flashcards.front: What is YIELD CURVE and what do shapes indicate?
flashcards.back:
Yield Curve: Plot of bond yields vs maturity

NORMAL (upward): Economy healthy
FLAT: Transition/uncertainty
INVERTED: Recession warning
HUMPED: Mid-term uncertainty

Long rates normally > short rates

flashcards.front: What are MASLOW\
flashcards.back:
From base to top:
1. PHYSIOLOGICAL - Food, shelter
2. SAFETY - Security, stability
3. BELONGING - Love, community
4. ESTEEM - Achievement, respect
5. SELF-ACTUALIZATION - Purpose

Lower needs must be met first

flashcards.front: What are UGMA/UTMA accounts?
flashcards.back:
Uniform Gifts/Transfers to Minors:
• Custodial account for minors
• Irrevocable gift
• First $1,300 unearned income tax-free
• Next $1,300 at child

flashcards.front: What are the main FEDERAL STUDENT LOAN repayment plans?
flashcards.back:
Repayment Plans:
• STANDARD - 10 years fixed
• GRADUATED - Starts low, increases
• EXTENDED - Up to 25 years
• INCOME-DRIVEN (IBR, PAYE, SAVE)
  - 10-25 years
  - % of discretionary income
  - Forgiveness at end

flashcards.front: What is PUBLIC SERVICE LOAN FORGIVENESS (PSLF)?
flashcards.back:
PSLF Requirements:
• 120 qualifying payments
• While working full-time public service
• Direct loans only
• Income-driven repayment plan
• Tax-free forgiveness
• 10 years of payments

flashcards.front: What are closing costs for homebuyers?
flashcards.back:
Typical Closing Costs (2-5% of purchase):
• Appraisal fee
• Title insurance
• Attorney fees
• Recording fees
• Origination/points
• Prepaid interest
• Escrow deposits (taxes/insurance)
• Home inspection

flashcards.front: What is PMI and when is it required?
flashcards.back:
PMI = Private Mortgage Insurance

Required when:
• Down payment <20%
• Conventional loan

Removal:
• Automatic at 78% LTV
• Request at 80% LTV
• Refinance when equity sufficient

FHA: MIP cannot be removed

flashcards.front: What is SERIAL PAYMENT calculation?
flashcards.back:
Serial Payment: When payment increases each year (often by inflation)

Use real rate of return:
Real rate = [(1+nominal)/(1+inflation)] - 1

Example: 8% return, 3% inflation
Real rate = (1.08/1.03) - 1 = 4.85%

Then solve for PMT using real rate

flashcards.front: What is UNEVEN CASH FLOW calculation?
flashcards.back:
Uneven Cash Flows:
• Use CF register on calculator
• Enter CF0 (usually negative investment)
• Enter each CF with frequency
• Compute NPV at given rate
• Or compute IRR

Used for: Projects, investment analysis

flashcards.front: What is included on a PERSONAL BALANCE SHEET?
flashcards.back:
ASSETS:
• Liquid (cash, checking, savings)
• Investment (stocks, bonds, retirement)
• Use (home, vehicles, personal property)

LIABILITIES:
• Short-term (credit cards, bills)
• Long-term (mortgage, student loans)

NET WORTH = Assets - Liabilities
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What is a PERSONAL CASH FLOW STATEMENT?
flashcards.back:
Cash Flow Statement shows:

INCOME:
• Salary, wages
• Interest, dividends
• Other income

EXPENSES:
• Fixed (rent, insurance)
• Variable (food, entertainment)

NET CASH FLOW = Income - Expenses

flashcards.front: What factors comprise a FICO CREDIT SCORE?
flashcards.back:
FICO Score Components:
• Payment History: 35%
• Amounts Owed: 30%
• Length of Credit: 15%
• Credit Mix: 10%
• New Credit: 10%

Range: 300-850
Good: 670+, Excellent: 800+

flashcards.front: What are the THREE types of income for tax purposes?
flashcards.back:
1. ORDINARY INCOME
   • Wages, interest, IRA distributions
   • Taxed at marginal rates

2. PORTFOLIO INCOME
   • Long-term capital gains, qualified dividends
   • Lower rates (0%, 15%, 20%)

3. PASSIVE INCOME
   • Rental, limited partnerships
   • Special loss rules
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: What is DOLLAR COST AVERAGING?
flashcards.back:
Dollar Cost Averaging:
• Invest fixed amount regularly
• Buy more shares when prices low
• Buy fewer shares when prices high
• Reduces average cost per share
• Eliminates market timing
• Good for systematic investing

flashcards.front: What is the difference between SYSTEMATIC and UNSYSTEMATIC risk?
flashcards.back:
SYSTEMATIC (Market) Risk:
• Affects entire market
• Cannot be diversified away
• Examples: Inflation, recession, interest rates
• Measured by beta

UNSYSTEMATIC (Specific) Risk:
• Company/industry specific
• CAN be diversified away
• Examples: Management, competition
flashcards.example: Stock with beta 1.2: if market rises 10%, expect stock to rise 12%. More volatile than market.

flashcards.front: What is OVERCONFIDENCE bias?
flashcards.back:
Overconfidence Bias:
• Overestimate abilities/knowledge
• Trade too frequently
• Take on too much risk
• Ignore contrary evidence

Manifests as:
• Excessive trading
• Concentrated positions
• Unrealistic return expectations

flashcards.front: What is HERDING behavior?
flashcards.back:
Herding: Following the crowd in investment decisions.

Examples:
• Buying during market bubbles
• Panic selling in crashes
• Following trends without analysis

Consequences:
• Buy high, sell low
• Miss contrarian opportunities

flashcards.front: What is FRAMING effect?
flashcards.back:
Framing Effect: Decisions affected by how choices are presented.

Examples:
• "90% success rate" vs "10% failure"
• "Save $100" vs "Avoid $100 loss"
• Positive vs negative presentation

Same info, different reactions

flashcards.front: What are the CFP Board\
flashcards.back:
Duties to Clients:
1. Fiduciary duty
2. Integrity
3. Competence
4. Diligence
5. Disclose and manage conflicts
6. Sound judgment
7. Professionalism
8. Comply with law
9. Confidentiality
flashcards.example: CFP professional managing $500,000 must recommend lowest-cost index funds if appropriate, not high-commission products.

flashcards.front: When can a CFP® disclose CONFIDENTIAL information?
flashcards.back:
Disclosure permitted when:
• Client consents
• Required by law
• Defend against charges
• In compliance with CFP Board
• Civil dispute with client

Must protect info otherwise

flashcards.front: What records must CFPs MAINTAIN?
flashcards.back:
Required Documentation:
• Client agreements
• Financial planning analysis
• Recommendations made
• Products implemented
• Correspondence/notes
• Disclosures provided

Retention: Per firm/regulatory requirements

flashcards.front: What is the difference between NOMINAL and EFFECTIVE interest rates?
flashcards.back:
NOMINAL (Stated) Rate:
• Annual rate as quoted
• Does not account for compounding

EFFECTIVE (APY) Rate:
• True rate with compounding
• EAR = (1 + r/n)^n - 1

More frequent compounding = higher effective rate

flashcards.front: What are the TYPES OF INTEREST calculations?
flashcards.back:
SIMPLE INTEREST:
I = P × r × t

COMPOUND INTEREST:
FV = P(1 + r)^n

Key difference:
• Simple: Interest on principal only
• Compound: Interest on interest
flashcards.example: Monthly compounding: $10,000 at 6% annual rate compounded monthly for 5 years: FV = $10,000 × (1 + 0.06/12)^60 = $13,489.

flashcards.front: What are the KEY RATIOS for personal financial health?
flashcards.back:
Key Personal Ratios:
• Emergency Fund: 3-6 months expenses
• Savings Rate: 10-15% minimum
• Housing: ≤28% gross income
• Total Debt: ≤36% gross income
• Net Worth: Growing over time
• Debt-to-Asset: <50%
flashcards.example: 3-6 months expenses. Client spends $5,000/month: maintain $15,000-$30,000 in liquid savings.

flashcards.front: What makes a financial goal SMART?
flashcards.back:
SMART Goals:
• Specific - Clear and defined
• Measurable - Quantifiable
• Achievable - Realistic
• Relevant - Aligned with values
• Time-bound - Deadline set

Example: "Save $50,000 for down payment in 3 years"
flashcards.example: Save $50,000 for down payment in 3 years

flashcards.front: Where should EMERGENCY FUNDS be held?
flashcards.back:
Emergency Fund Locations:
• High-yield savings account
• Money market account
• Short-term CDs (laddered)
• Treasury bills

Priorities:
• Safety of principal
• Liquidity
• Modest return
• NO market risk
flashcards.example: 3-6 months expenses. Client spends $5,000/month: maintain $15,000-$30,000 in liquid savings.

flashcards.front: Compare DEBT AVALANCHE vs DEBT SNOWBALL methods
flashcards.back:
DEBT AVALANCHE:
• Pay highest interest first
• Mathematically optimal
• Saves most money

DEBT SNOWBALL:
• Pay smallest balance first
• Psychological wins
• Builds momentum

Both: Make minimum on all, extra on target

flashcards.front: What are the TAX BENEFITS of home ownership?
flashcards.back:
Home Tax Benefits:
• Mortgage interest deduction (up to $750K)
• State/local property tax (up to $10K SALT)
• Capital gains exclusion ($250K/$500K)
• Home office deduction (if qualify)

Note: Must itemize to claim most

flashcards.front: What is the HOME SALE CAPITAL GAIN EXCLUSION?
flashcards.back:
Section 121 Exclusion:
• Single: $250,000
• Married filing joint: $500,000

Requirements:
• Owned 2 of last 5 years
• Lived in 2 of last 5 years
• Haven

flashcards.front: What is DEFLATION vs DISINFLATION?
flashcards.back:
DEFLATION:
• Falling prices (negative inflation)
• Can trigger economic crisis
• Delays spending

DISINFLATION:
• Slowing rate of inflation
• Prices still rising, just slower
• Usually positive sign

flashcards.front: What is STAGFLATION?
flashcards.back:
Stagflation:
• Stagnant economic growth
• High inflation
• High unemployment

Difficult to address:
• Fighting inflation raises unemployment
• Stimulating growth increases inflation

Example: 1970s oil crisis

flashcards.front: What is the SEC and what does it regulate?
flashcards.back:
Securities and Exchange Commission:
• Federal securities regulator
• Protects investors
• Maintains fair markets
• Facilitates capital formation

Regulates:
• Securities offerings
• Investment advisers
• Broker-dealers
• Exchanges

flashcards.front: What is FINRA?
flashcards.back:
Financial Industry Regulatory Authority:
• Self-regulatory organization
• Regulates broker-dealers
• Writes/enforces rules
• Examines firms
• Licenses representatives (Series exams)
• Investor dispute resolution

flashcards.front: How to calculate INFLATION-ADJUSTED returns?
flashcards.back:
Inflation-Adjusted Return:

Fisher Formula:
(1 + nominal) / (1 + inflation) - 1

Example:
• Nominal return: 8%
• Inflation: 3%
• Real return: (1.08/1.03) - 1 = 4.85%

Approximate: 8% - 3% = 5%

flashcards.front: What protections does FDIC provide?
flashcards.back:
FDIC Insurance:
• $250,000 per depositor per bank
• Covers checking, savings, CDs, MMDAs
• NOT: stocks, bonds, mutual funds, life insurance

Multiple accounts at same bank:
• Different ownership categories can increase coverage
flashcards.example: Term: $1M coverage, 20-year term, $50/month. Whole life: $500,000 coverage, $400/month, builds cash value.

flashcards.front: What is SIPC protection?
flashcards.back:
Securities Investor Protection Corporation:
• Protects if broker-dealer fails
• Up to $500,000 (incl. $250K cash)
• Does NOT protect against market loss
• Covers securities and cash
• Not a government agency

flashcards.front: How do you solve for UNKNOWN INTEREST RATE?
flashcards.back:
Calculator Steps:
• Enter PV (as negative)
• Enter FV (as positive)
• Enter N (periods)
• Enter PMT if annuity (or 0)
• Compute I/Y

Example: $1,000 grows to $1,500 in 5 years
PV=-1000, FV=1500, N=5, CPT I/Y = 8.45%

flashcards.front: How do you solve for UNKNOWN TIME PERIOD?
flashcards.back:
Calculator Steps:
• Enter PV (as negative)
• Enter FV (as positive)
• Enter I/Y (rate)
• Enter PMT if annuity (or 0)
• Compute N

Example: Double money at 7%
PV=-100, FV=200, I/Y=7, CPT N = 10.24 years
(Rule of 72: 72/7 ≈ 10.3)

flashcards.front: CFP Financial Planning Process - 7 Steps
flashcards.back:
Understanding client circumstances
Identifying and selecting goals
Analyzing current course
Developing recommendations
Presenting recommendations
Implementing recommendations
Monitoring progress

(Remember: U I A D P I M)

flashcards.front: CFP Code of Ethics - Eight Standards
flashcards.back:
Act with integrity
Competence required
Treat information confidentially

Fiduciary duty (always material steps)
Act with diligence
Inform clients of conflicts
Responsibility to profession

CFP Board can sanction for violations
flashcards.example: CFP professional managing $500,000 must recommend lowest-cost index funds if appropriate, not high-commission products.

flashcards.front: CFP Certification Requirements
flashcards.back:
Education (CFP Board-registered program)
Examination (6-hour CFP exam)
Experience (6,000 hours, or 4,000 with supervision)
Ethics (background check, declaration)

All four required for CFP certification

flashcards.front: What are the MAJOR ASSET CLASSES?
flashcards.back:
Major Asset Classes:
• EQUITIES: Stocks, equity funds
• FIXED INCOME: Bonds, CDs, treasuries
• CASH EQUIVALENTS: Money market, T-bills
• REAL ESTATE: Property, REITs
• COMMODITIES: Gold, oil, agriculture
• ALTERNATIVES: Hedge funds, PE, crypto

Each has different risk/return profiles

flashcards.front: What is the EQUITY RISK PREMIUM?
flashcards.back:
Equity Risk Premium (ERP):
• Extra return expected from stocks over risk-free rate
• Historical: ~5-7% annually
• Compensation for equity volatility

Expected Return = Risk-Free Rate + ERP

Higher ERP = More attractive stocks
Varies with market conditions

flashcards.front: What is MODERN PORTFOLIO THEORY (MPT)?
flashcards.back:
Modern Portfolio Theory (Markowitz):
• Diversification reduces portfolio risk
• Investors are risk-averse
• Optimal portfolios on efficient frontier

Key Concepts:
• Expected return
• Standard deviation (risk)
• Correlation between assets
• Risk-free rate

Goal: Maximum return for given risk level
flashcards.example: Portfolio: US large cap 30%, US small cap 10%, international 15%, emerging markets 5%, bonds 35%, REITs 5%.

flashcards.front: What is the EFFICIENT FRONTIER?
flashcards.back:
Efficient Frontier:
• Curve of optimal portfolios
• Maximum return for each risk level
• Portfolios below are sub-optimal
• Cannot achieve above without more risk

Moving along frontier:
• Right = Higher risk, higher return
• Left = Lower risk, lower return

All rational investors choose frontier portfolios

flashcards.front: What is STANDARD DEVIATION in investing?
flashcards.back:
Standard Deviation (σ):
• Measures dispersion of returns
• Higher σ = Higher volatility/risk
• Most common risk measure

Distribution:
• 68% within ±1 σ
• 95% within ±2 σ
• 99.7% within ±3 σ

Measures TOTAL risk (systematic + unsystematic)
flashcards.example: Fund A: 10% return, 15% std dev. Fund B: 10% return, 8% std dev. Fund B offers same return with less volatility (risk).

flashcards.front: What is BETA?
flashcards.back:
Beta (β):
• Measures systematic (market) risk
• Sensitivity to market movements

Interpretation:
• β = 1.0: Moves with market
• β > 1.0: More volatile than market
• β < 1.0: Less volatile than market
• β < 0: Moves opposite to market

Beta of market = 1.0
Cannot diversify away systematic risk
flashcards.example: Stock with beta 1.2: if market rises 10%, expect stock to rise 12%. More volatile than market.

flashcards.front: What is ALPHA?
flashcards.back:
Alpha (α):
• Excess return over expected (CAPM)
• Measures manager skill

Positive Alpha:
• Outperformed risk-adjusted benchmark
• "Beat the market"

Negative Alpha:
• Underperformed

Formula: Actual Return - [Rf + β(Rm - Rf)]

After fees, most funds have negative alpha
flashcards.example: Portfolio returned 12%, expected return (based on beta) was 10%. Alpha = 2%, indicating manager outperformance.

flashcards.front: What is the SHARPE RATIO?
flashcards.back:
Sharpe Ratio:
• Risk-adjusted return measure
• Return per unit of TOTAL risk

Formula: (Rp - Rf) / σp

Where:
• Rp = Portfolio return
• Rf = Risk-free rate
• σp = Standard deviation

Higher = Better risk-adjusted performance
Compare investments with different risks
flashcards.example: Fund A: 10% return, 15% std dev. Fund B: 10% return, 8% std dev. Fund B offers same return with less volatility (risk).

flashcards.front: What is the TREYNOR RATIO?
flashcards.back:
Treynor Ratio:
• Risk-adjusted return measure
• Return per unit of SYSTEMATIC risk

Formula: (Rp - Rf) / βp

Difference from Sharpe:
• Uses beta not standard deviation
• Best for diversified portfolios
• Measures only market risk

Higher = Better systematic risk-adjusted return
flashcards.example: Fund A: 10% return, 15% std dev. Fund B: 10% return, 8% std dev. Fund B offers same return with less volatility (risk).

flashcards.front: What is JENSEN\
flashcards.back: Jensen

flashcards.front: What is the CAPITAL ASSET PRICING MODEL (CAPM)?
flashcards.back:
CAPM:
• Calculates required return based on risk

Formula: E(Ri) = Rf + βi(Rm - Rf)

Where:
• E(Ri) = Expected return
• Rf = Risk-free rate
• βi = Beta of asset
• Rm = Market return
• (Rm - Rf) = Market risk premium

Only systematic risk is compensated
flashcards.example: Stock with beta 1.2: if market rises 10%, expect stock to rise 12%. More volatile than market.

flashcards.front: What is the SECURITY MARKET LINE (SML)?
flashcards.back:
Security Market Line:
• Graphical representation of CAPM
• X-axis: Beta
• Y-axis: Expected return
• Y-intercept: Risk-free rate
• Slope: Market risk premium

Points above SML: Undervalued
Points below SML: Overvalued
On line: Fairly valued
flashcards.example: Stock with beta 1.2: if market rises 10%, expect stock to rise 12%. More volatile than market.

flashcards.front: What is the CAPITAL MARKET LINE (CML)?
flashcards.back:
Capital Market Line:
• Combines risk-free asset with market portfolio
• X-axis: Standard deviation (total risk)
• Y-axis: Expected return

Difference from SML:
• CML uses standard deviation
• SML uses beta
• CML only for efficient portfolios

Slope = Sharpe ratio of market portfolio
flashcards.example: Fund A: 10% return, 15% std dev. Fund B: 10% return, 8% std dev. Fund B offers same return with less volatility (risk).

flashcards.front: What is SYSTEMATIC vs UNSYSTEMATIC risk?
flashcards.back:
SYSTEMATIC (Market) Risk:
• Affects entire market
• Cannot diversify away
• Examples: Interest rates, inflation, recession
• Measured by beta

UNSYSTEMATIC (Specific) Risk:
• Affects individual security
• CAN diversify away
• Examples: Management, labor strikes, lawsuits

Total Risk = Systematic + Unsystematic
flashcards.example: Stock with beta 1.2: if market rises 10%, expect stock to rise 12%. More volatile than market.

flashcards.front: What is CORRELATION and its role in diversification?
flashcards.back:
Correlation Coefficient (ρ):
• Measures how assets move together
• Range: -1 to +1

Interpretation:
• +1: Perfect positive (no diversification)
• 0: No relationship
• -1: Perfect negative (max diversification)

Diversification benefits increase as correlation decreases
Ideal: Low or negative correlation
flashcards.example: Portfolio: US large cap 30%, US small cap 10%, international 15%, emerging markets 5%, bonds 35%, REITs 5%.

flashcards.front: How many stocks needed for ADEQUATE DIVERSIFICATION?
flashcards.back:
Diversification Research:
• 15-20 stocks eliminates ~90% unsystematic risk
• 30+ stocks for further reduction
• Diminishing returns after 40-50

Important factors:
• Low correlation between holdings
• Different sectors/industries
• Various market caps
• Geographic diversification

Cannot eliminate systematic risk
flashcards.example: Portfolio: US large cap 30%, US small cap 10%, international 15%, emerging markets 5%, bonds 35%, REITs 5%.

flashcards.front: What is BOND DURATION?
flashcards.back:
Duration:
• Weighted average time to receive cash flows
• Measures price sensitivity to interest rates

Macaulay Duration:
• Expressed in years

Modified Duration:
• Price sensitivity measure
• % price change for 1% rate change

Higher duration = Greater interest rate risk
Zero-coupon has highest duration = maturity

flashcards.front: What is BOND CONVEXITY?
flashcards.back:
Convexity:
• Measures curvature in price-yield relationship
• Duration only approximates (linear)
• Convexity captures non-linear portion

Benefits of Positive Convexity:
• Price rises MORE than duration predicts when rates fall
• Price falls LESS than duration predicts when rates rise

Callable bonds have negative convexity

flashcards.front: What is the YIELD CURVE?
flashcards.back:
Yield Curve:
• Graph of yields across maturities
• Same credit quality (usually Treasuries)

Shapes:
• NORMAL: Upward sloping (short < long)
• INVERTED: Downward sloping (short > long)
• FLAT: Similar rates across maturities

Inverted curve historically predicts recession
Steep curve: Expect rate increases

flashcards.front: What are BOND RATING categories?
flashcards.back:
Bond Ratings:

INVESTMENT GRADE:
• AAA/Aaa: Highest quality
• AA/Aa: High quality
• A/A: Upper medium
• BBB/Baa: Medium grade

SPECULATIVE (Junk):
• BB/Ba: Speculative
• B/B: Highly speculative
• CCC-C: Substantial risk
• D: Default

S&P/Fitch use letters; Moody

flashcards.front: What are TYPES of corporate bonds?
flashcards.back:
Corporate Bond Types:
• SECURED: Backed by collateral
  - Mortgage bonds (real estate)
  - Equipment trust (equipment)
• UNSECURED (Debentures): General credit
• SUBORDINATED: Junior to other debt
• CONVERTIBLE: Convert to stock
• CALLABLE: Issuer can redeem early
• PUTABLE: Holder can sell back
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What are MUNICIPAL BOND types?
flashcards.back:
Municipal Bonds:

GENERAL OBLIGATION (GO):
• Backed by taxing power
• Full faith and credit

REVENUE BONDS:
• Backed by specific project revenue
• Higher risk, higher yield

Both:
• Federal tax-exempt
• State tax-exempt (if in-state)
• May trigger AMT (private activity)

flashcards.front: What are TREASURY securities?
flashcards.back:
Treasury Securities:

T-BILLS:
• <1 year maturity
• Sold at discount, no coupon

T-NOTES:
• 2-10 year maturity
• Semiannual coupons

T-BONDS:
• 20-30 year maturity
• Semiannual coupons

TIPS:
• Inflation-protected
• Principal adjusts with CPI

All: Federal tax, state tax-exempt

flashcards.front: What is the DIVIDEND DISCOUNT MODEL (DDM)?
flashcards.back:
Dividend Discount Model:
• Stock value = PV of future dividends

GORDON GROWTH MODEL:
P₀ = D₁ / (r - g)

Where:
• D₁ = Next year

flashcards.front: What is the P/E RATIO?
flashcards.back:
Price-to-Earnings Ratio:
• P/E = Price per Share / Earnings per Share

Types:
• TRAILING P/E: Past 12 months earnings
• FORWARD P/E: Projected earnings

Interpretation:
• High P/E: Growth expectations or overvalued
• Low P/E: Value or problems

Compare to industry and historical averages

flashcards.front: What is PRICE-TO-BOOK ratio?
flashcards.back:
Price-to-Book Ratio:
• P/B = Market Price / Book Value per Share
• Book Value = Assets - Liabilities

Interpretation:
• P/B < 1: Trading below book value
• P/B > 1: Premium to book value

Best for:
• Financial companies
• Asset-heavy industries

Limitation: Ignores intangibles

flashcards.front: What is DISCOUNTED CASH FLOW (DCF) valuation?
flashcards.back:
Discounted Cash Flow:
• Value = PV of future free cash flows

Steps:
1. Project future cash flows
2. Estimate terminal value
3. Discount to present value
4. Sum = Intrinsic value

Discount Rate: WACC or required return

Advantage: Doesn
flashcards.example: To have $50,000 in 10 years at 5% return: PV = $50,000 / (1.05)^10 = $30,696 needed today.

flashcards.front: What is VALUE vs GROWTH investing?
flashcards.back:
VALUE INVESTING:
• Buy undervalued stocks
• Low P/E, P/B ratios
• Higher dividend yields
• Margin of safety focus

GROWTH INVESTING:
• Buy stocks with high growth potential
• Higher P/E, P/B ratios
• Reinvest earnings
• Future earnings focus

Value tends to outperform long-term

flashcards.front: What is MARKET CAP classification?
flashcards.back:
Market Capitalization:
• Market Cap = Price × Shares Outstanding

Categories:
• MEGA CAP: >$200B
• LARGE CAP: $10B-$200B
• MID CAP: $2B-$10B
• SMALL CAP: $300M-$2B
• MICRO CAP: $50M-$300M

Smaller = Higher risk, higher potential return
Size premium exists historically

flashcards.front: Compare OPEN-END vs CLOSED-END funds
flashcards.back:
OPEN-END (Mutual Funds):
• Continuously issue/redeem shares
• Trade at NAV
• Priced end of day
• Unlimited shares

CLOSED-END:
• Fixed number of shares
• Trade on exchange
• Can trade at premium or discount to NAV
• Intraday pricing
• May use leverage

flashcards.front: What are ETF advantages over mutual funds?
flashcards.back:
ETF Advantages:
• Tax efficiency (in-kind creation/redemption)
• Lower expense ratios
• Intraday trading
• Transparency (holdings disclosed daily)
• No minimum investment
• Can short, use limit orders

Disadvantages:
• Trading commissions
• Bid-ask spreads
• Premium/discount to NAV
flashcards.example: Index fund: 0.05% ER. Active fund: 1.2% ER. On $100,000, difference is $1,150/year in fees.

flashcards.front: What is a UNIT INVESTMENT TRUST (UIT)?
flashcards.back:
Unit Investment Trust:
• Fixed portfolio of securities
• No active management
• Termination date
• Self-liquidating
• Redeemable at NAV
• Low expenses

Types:
• Equity UITs
• Bond UITs

Compare to ETF: Similar but not traded, has maturity
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What is a REIT?
flashcards.back:
Real Estate Investment Trust:
• Owns/operates income-producing real estate
• Trade on exchanges like stocks
• Must distribute 90% taxable income
• Pass-through taxation

Types:
• EQUITY REITs: Own property
• MORTGAGE REITs: Own mortgages
• HYBRID: Both

Correlation: Low with stocks, bonds
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What are basic CALL and PUT options?
flashcards.back:
OPTIONS:

CALL OPTION:
• Right to BUY at strike price
• Bullish on underlying
• Buy call: Max loss = premium
• Sell call: Max loss = unlimited

PUT OPTION:
• Right to SELL at strike price
• Bearish on underlying
• Buy put: Max loss = premium
• Sell put: Max loss = strike - premium

flashcards.front: What is COVERED CALL strategy?
flashcards.back:
Covered Call:
• Own stock + Sell call option

Goals:
• Generate income (premium)
• Slight downside protection
• Exit strategy

Risk/Reward:
• Limited upside (capped at strike)
• Downside risk remains (less premium)
• Best for neutral to slightly bullish

Not speculative - income oriented

flashcards.front: What is PROTECTIVE PUT strategy?
flashcards.back:
Protective Put:
• Own stock + Buy put option
• Portfolio insurance

Goals:
• Limit downside risk
• Maintain upside potential
• Like buying insurance

Cost: Premium paid
• Reduces overall return
• Floor on losses

Best: Uncertain market, want to hold position

flashcards.front: What is INTRINSIC vs TIME VALUE?
flashcards.back:
Option Value:

INTRINSIC VALUE:
• In-the-money amount
• Call: Stock price - Strike price
• Put: Strike price - Stock price
• Cannot be negative

TIME VALUE:
• Premium - Intrinsic value
• Based on time remaining
• Decays as expiration approaches
• Theta measures decay rate

Total Premium = Intrinsic + Time Value
flashcards.example: Investing $10,000 at 7% for 10 years: FV = $10,000 × (1.07)^10 = $19,672. The $9,672 gain represents the time value of money.

flashcards.front: What is STRATEGIC vs TACTICAL asset allocation?
flashcards.back:
STRATEGIC (LONG-TERM):
• Base policy allocation
• Set based on goals, risk, horizon
• Rebalance to maintain
• Buy and hold approach

TACTICAL (SHORT-TERM):
• Deviations from strategic
• Exploit market opportunities
• Active approach
• Ranges around strategic weights

Core-satellite combines both
flashcards.example: Moderate risk tolerance: 60% stocks (domestic/international), 35% bonds, 5% cash. Rebalance quarterly.

flashcards.front: What is REBALANCING and why is it important?
flashcards.back:
Rebalancing:
• Restoring portfolio to target allocation
• After market movements cause drift

Methods:
• Calendar: Periodic (quarterly, annually)
• Threshold: When deviation exceeds %

Benefits:
• Maintains risk level
• Forces "sell high, buy low"
• Discipline

Costs: Taxes, transaction costs

flashcards.front: What is EFFICIENT MARKET HYPOTHESIS (EMH)?
flashcards.back:
Efficient Market Hypothesis:
• Prices reflect all available information
• Cannot consistently beat market

Forms:
• WEAK: Past prices reflected
• SEMI-STRONG: All public info reflected
• STRONG: All info (including private)

Implications: Passive investing, index funds
Criticism: Bubbles, anomalies exist

flashcards.front: What are MARKET ANOMALIES?
flashcards.back:
Market Anomalies:
• Persistent patterns inconsistent with EMH

Examples:
• SIZE EFFECT: Small caps outperform
• VALUE EFFECT: Low P/B outperforms
• MOMENTUM: Winners keep winning
• JANUARY EFFECT: Small cap January gains
• WEEKDAY EFFECT: Monday returns lower

Debate: Real alpha or risk factors?
flashcards.example: Portfolio returned 12%, expected return (based on beta) was 10%. Alpha = 2%, indicating manager outperformance.

flashcards.front: What are HEDGE FUND characteristics?
flashcards.back:
Hedge Funds:
• Alternative investment vehicle
• Various strategies (long/short, arbitrage)
• Accredited investors only
• Limited liquidity (lock-ups)
• 2 and 20 fee structure
• Less regulated
• Absolute return focus

Strategies:
• Long/short equity
• Market neutral
• Global macro
• Event-driven

flashcards.front: What is PRIVATE EQUITY?
flashcards.back:
Private Equity:
• Investment in private companies
• Long holding periods (5-10 years)
• Illiquid
• J-curve returns

Types:
• BUYOUTS: Acquire mature companies
• VENTURE CAPITAL: Early-stage companies
• GROWTH EQUITY: Expansion capital

Fees: 2% management, 20% carry
Minimum: Typically $250K+

flashcards.front: What BOND RISKS should investors know?
flashcards.back:
Bond Risks:
• INTEREST RATE: Prices fall when rates rise
• CREDIT/DEFAULT: Issuer may not pay
• REINVESTMENT: Lower rates on reinvestment
• INFLATION: Purchasing power erosion
• CALL: Called before maturity
• LIQUIDITY: Hard to sell
• CURRENCY: Foreign bond exposure

Duration helps quantify interest rate risk

flashcards.front: What is a STOCK SPLIT and REVERSE SPLIT?
flashcards.back:
STOCK SPLIT:
• Increases shares, reduces price proportionally
• Example: 2-for-1 doubles shares, halves price
• No change in value
• Improves liquidity, accessibility

REVERSE SPLIT:
• Reduces shares, increases price
• Example: 1-for-10 reduces shares by 90%
• Often to meet listing requirements
• May signal trouble

flashcards.front: What is TIME-WEIGHTED vs DOLLAR-WEIGHTED return?
flashcards.back:
TIME-WEIGHTED RETURN (TWR):
• Eliminates cash flow effects
• Standard for measuring managers
• Geometric linking of sub-periods
• Ignores client deposits/withdrawals

DOLLAR-WEIGHTED (IRR):
• Considers timing of cash flows
• Reflects investor experience
• Personal rate of return

Use TWR to evaluate managers

flashcards.front: What is INFORMATION RATIO?
flashcards.back:
Information Ratio:
• Active return per unit of tracking error
• Measures consistency of outperformance

Formula: (Rp - Rb) / Tracking Error

Where:
• Rp = Portfolio return
• Rb = Benchmark return
• Tracking Error = Std dev of difference

Higher = Better active management
Typically: 0.5 good, 1.0 excellent

flashcards.front: What is SORTINO RATIO?
flashcards.back:
Sortino Ratio:
• Modified Sharpe ratio
• Only penalizes downside deviation

Formula: (Rp - Target) / Downside Deviation

Difference from Sharpe:
• Sharpe uses total volatility
• Sortino only uses bad volatility
• Better for asymmetric returns

Higher = Better risk-adjusted downside performance
flashcards.example: Portfolio: 12% return, 3% risk-free rate, 15% std dev. Sharpe = (12% - 3%) / 15% = 0.60. Higher is better.

flashcards.front: What is a PROSPECTUS?
flashcards.back:
Prospectus:
• Legal document describing investment
• Required for securities offerings
• Contains:
  - Investment objectives
  - Risks
  - Fees and expenses
  - Management info
  - Historical performance
• Must receive before or with purchase
• Summary prospectus allowed for funds

flashcards.front: What is FINRA\
flashcards.back:
FINRA (Financial Industry Regulatory Authority):
• Self-regulatory organization
• Oversees broker-dealers
• Writes and enforces rules
• Licenses reps (Series exams)
• Arbitration disputes
• NOT a government agency

Key rules:
• Suitability
• Best execution
• Know your customer
flashcards.example: Recommending aggressive growth fund to 75-year-old retiree seeking income would violate suitability standards.

flashcards.front: What are LIMIT vs MARKET orders?
flashcards.back:
MARKET ORDER:
• Execute immediately at current price
• Guaranteed execution
• No price guarantee
• Best for liquid securities

LIMIT ORDER:
• Execute only at specified price or better
• Price guarantee
• No execution guarantee
• May be partially filled

Buy limit: At or below
Sell limit: At or above

flashcards.front: What is a STOP-LOSS order?
flashcards.back:
Stop-Loss Order:
• Becomes market order when trigger hit
• Limit potential losses

Sell Stop: Below current price
• Protects long position

Buy Stop: Above current price
• Protects short position

STOP-LIMIT:
• Becomes limit order at trigger
• Price guarantee but may not fill

Risk: Gap through stop price

flashcards.front: What is MARGIN trading?
flashcards.back:
Margin Trading:
• Borrow to buy securities
• Leverage increases returns and losses

Regulation T:
• Initial margin: 50% cash
• Maintenance: 25-30% equity

MARGIN CALL:
• Triggered when equity falls below maintenance
• Must deposit cash or sell securities

Risk: Losses can exceed investment

flashcards.front: What are SUPPORT and RESISTANCE levels?
flashcards.back:
SUPPORT:
• Price floor where buying increases
• Demand exceeds supply
• Bounce expected
• Break below is bearish

RESISTANCE:
• Price ceiling where selling increases
• Supply exceeds demand
• Pullback expected
• Break above is bullish

Prior support becomes resistance and vice versa

flashcards.front: What are MOVING AVERAGES?
flashcards.back:
Moving Averages:
• Smooth price data over time
• Identify trends

SIMPLE (SMA): Equal weight
EXPONENTIAL (EMA): More weight to recent

Common periods: 50, 100, 200 day

Signals:
• GOLDEN CROSS: 50 crosses above 200 (bullish)
• DEATH CROSS: 50 crosses below 200 (bearish)

Lagging indicator

flashcards.front: Capital Asset Pricing Model
flashcards.back:
Expected Return = Risk-free rate + β(Market return - Risk-free rate)

β = Systematic risk measure
• β = 1: Same as market
• β > 1: More volatile
• β < 1: Less volatile

Only compensated for systematic risk

flashcards.front: Security Market Line vs Capital Market Line
flashcards.back:
SML (Security Market Line):
• Y-axis: Expected return
• X-axis: Beta (systematic risk)
• Graphs individual securities

CML (Capital Market Line):
• Y-axis: Expected return
• X-axis: Standard deviation (total risk)
• Graphs efficient portfolios only

SML uses CAPM; CML uses Sharpe ratio
flashcards.example: Fund A: 10% return, 15% std dev. Fund B: 10% return, 8% std dev. Fund B offers same return with less volatility (risk).

flashcards.front: Treynor Ratio
flashcards.back:
Treynor = (Portfolio Return - RF) ÷ Beta

Measures excess return per unit of SYSTEMATIC risk

Compare to:
• Sharpe: Uses standard deviation (total risk)
• Jensen: Uses alpha (abnormal return)

Higher Treynor = better risk-adjusted return
flashcards.example: Fund A: 10% return, 15% std dev. Fund B: 10% return, 8% std dev. Fund B offers same return with less volatility (risk).

flashcards.front: Duration and Bond Price Sensitivity
flashcards.back:
Price Change ≈ -Duration × Δ Interest Rate

• Duration 5 + 1% rate increase = ~5% price drop
• Longer duration = more price sensitivity
• Higher coupon = lower duration
• Longer maturity = higher duration

Modified duration adjusts for payment frequency

flashcards.front: What are the FOUR Es of CFP certification?
flashcards.back: CFP Certification Requirements:\n\n1. EDUCATION: Bachelor

flashcards.front: What is the CFP BOARD\
flashcards.back:
CFP Board Disciplinary Process:

1. COMPLAINT: Filed with CFP Board
2. INVESTIGATION: Staff review
3. PROBABLE CAUSE: Determination if violation
4. HEARING: Before Disciplinary and Ethics Commission
5. DECISION: Sanctions applied

Sanctions:
• Private censure
• Public letter
• Suspension
• Revocation

Appeals possible

flashcards.front: When does FIDUCIARY duty apply to CFP professionals?
flashcards.back:
CFP Fiduciary Duty:

ALWAYS applies when:
• Providing financial advice
• A client reasonably relies on advice

Duty of Loyalty:
• Act in client
flashcards.example: CFP professional managing $500,000 must recommend lowest-cost index funds if appropriate, not high-commission products.

flashcards.front: What is the DUTY OF LOYALTY?
flashcards.back: Duty of Loyalty:\n• Act in client

flashcards.front: What is the DUTY OF CARE?
flashcards.back:
Duty of Care:
• Act with skill, competence, diligence

Components:
• Understand client

flashcards.front: What must be included in a CLIENT ENGAGEMENT?
flashcards.back:
Client Engagement Requirements:

Must Specify:
• Scope of engagement
• Services to be provided
• How advice will be provided
• Client and professional responsibilities
• Compensation and conflicts
• Duration/termination

Can be written or oral (written preferred)
Confirm in writing if oral

flashcards.front: What are SCOPE OF ENGAGEMENT considerations?
flashcards.back:
Scope Considerations:

May Limit Scope When:
• Client agrees
• Limitation is reasonable
• Written disclosure provided
• Clearly communicated

Cannot Limit:
• Fiduciary duty (always applies)
• Practice Standards (when doing planning)
• Ethical obligations

Limited scope example: Investment-only engagement
flashcards.example: CFP professional managing $500,000 must recommend lowest-cost index funds if appropriate, not high-commission products.

flashcards.front: What are the 7 steps of FINANCIAL PLANNING process?
flashcards.back:
Financial Planning Process (7 Steps):

1. Understanding client"s personal and financial circumstances
2. Identifying and selecting goals
3. Analyzing current course of action
4. Developing recommendations
5. Presenting recommendations
6. Implementing recommendations
7. Monitoring progress and updating

Iterative process - may revisit steps

flashcards.front: What information must be gathered about a CLIENT?
flashcards.back:
Client Data Gathering:

QUANTITATIVE:
• Income, expenses, cash flow
• Assets, liabilities, net worth
• Tax returns, insurance policies
• Investment statements

QUALITATIVE:
• Goals and objectives
• Values and beliefs
• Risk tolerance
• Time horizons
• Family situation
• Health considerations
flashcards.example: Questionnaire scores: time horizon 10+ years (high), loss tolerance 15% (moderate), income stability high → moderate-aggressive allocation.

flashcards.front: How should CONFLICTS OF INTEREST be handled?
flashcards.back:
Conflict Management:

1. IDENTIFY: Recognize all conflicts
2. AVOID: If possible/practical
3. DISCLOSE: Material conflicts to client
4. MANAGE: If cannot avoid
5. DOCUMENT: Disclosure and consent

Material Conflicts:
• Compensation arrangements
• Proprietary products
• Outside business activities
• Personal relationships

flashcards.front: What COMPENSATION conflicts must be disclosed?
flashcards.back:
Compensation Disclosure:

Direct:
• Fees paid by client
• Commissions
• Flat fees, hourly, AUM

Indirect:
• 12b-1 fees
• Revenue sharing
• Referral fees
• Bonus structures

Must Disclose:
• Amount or method
• Who pays
• When paid
• Any changes

Client must understand incentives

flashcards.front: What are CFP® CONFIDENTIALITY obligations?
flashcards.back:
Confidentiality Rules:

Protect Client Information:
• Financial data
• Personal information
• Goals and circumstances

Exceptions Allow Disclosure:
• Client consents
• Legal/regulatory requirement
• Defend against charges
• Comply with CFP Board

Cannot use for personal benefit
Extends beyond client relationship

flashcards.front: What is the COMPETENCE requirement?
flashcards.back:
Competence Obligation:

Must have:
• Sufficient knowledge
• Skill and ability
• To provide advice competently

If lacking competence:
• Decline engagement
• Limit scope appropriately
• Associate with/refer to competent professional
• Acquire competence (with disclosure)

Ongoing: Stay current in profession

flashcards.front: What does INTEGRITY require of CFP® professionals?
flashcards.back:
Integrity Principle:

• Honesty in all dealings
• Avoid deceptive conduct
• Do not mislead
• Keep promises
• Act in good faith
• Be trustworthy

Applies to:
• Client interactions
• Professional relationships
• Public statements
• Regulatory filings

Foundation of public trust
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What does PROFESSIONALISM require?
flashcards.back:
Professionalism Requirements:

• Dignity and courtesy
• Respect for clients and others
• Uphold reputation of profession
• Comply with laws and regulations
• Cooperate with regulatory authorities
• Do not engage in disgraceful conduct

Extends beyond professional activities
Personal conduct matters

flashcards.front: What is the INVESTMENT ADVISERS ACT of 1940?
flashcards.back:
Investment Advisers Act:

Registration Required for advisers who:
• Provide investment advice
• As a business
• For compensation

Exemptions:
• Accountants/lawyers (incidental)
• Publishers
• Certain private advisers

SEC vs State:
• >$100M: SEC registration
• $25-100M: Can choose
• <$25M: State registration

flashcards.front: What is REGULATION BEST INTEREST (Reg BI)?
flashcards.back:
Reg BI (SEC Rule):

Applies to: Broker-dealers
Standard: Best interest (not suitability alone)

Four Obligations:
1. DISCLOSURE: CRS and transaction-specific
2. CARE: Reasonable basis, customer-specific
3. CONFLICTS: Identify and mitigate
4. COMPLIANCE: Policies and procedures

Enhanced but NOT pure fiduciary
Effective June 2020
flashcards.example: CFP professional managing $500,000 must recommend lowest-cost index funds if appropriate, not high-commission products.

flashcards.front: What is Form ADV?
flashcards.back:
Form ADV:

Investment Adviser Registration:

PART 1: Business information
• Ownership, affiliations
• Disciplinary history
• AUM, employees

PART 2A (Brochure): Client disclosure
• Services, fees
• Conflicts, disciplinary
• Background, custody

PART 2B (Supplement): Individual advisers

PART 3 (CRS): Relationship Summary

flashcards.front: What is Form CRS?
flashcards.back:
Form CRS (Client Relationship Summary):

Required by: SEC (Reg BI)
Max: 2 pages (4 for dual registrants)

Contents:
• Relationship and services
• Fees and costs
• Conflicts of interest
• Legal standard of conduct
• Disciplinary history
• Key questions to ask

Must deliver at/before recommendation
"Conversation starter" questions

flashcards.front: What is the difference between RIA and BD?
flashcards.back:
RIA vs Broker-Dealer:

RIA (Investment Adviser):
• Fiduciary duty
• Fee-based (generally)
• SEC or state registered
• Investment Advisers Act

BROKER-DEALER:
• Reg BI / Suitability
• Commission-based (generally)
• FINRA registered
• Securities Exchange Act

Dual Registration: Both
Hybrid: RIA using BD for transactions
flashcards.example: CFP professional managing $500,000 must recommend lowest-cost index funds if appropriate, not high-commission products.

flashcards.front: What are ERISA fiduciary responsibilities?
flashcards.back:
ERISA Fiduciary Duties:

Applies to: Retirement plan fiduciaries

Duties:
• Act solely in participant interest
• Prudent expert standard
• Diversify investments
• Follow plan documents
• Pay only reasonable expenses

Fiduciary = Anyone with control/authority
Personal liability for breaches
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What does REGULATION S-P require?
flashcards.back:
Regulation S-P (Privacy):

SEC rule for financial institutions:

• Initial privacy notice at relationship
• Annual privacy notice
• Opt-out for sharing with non-affiliates
• Safeguard customer information

Nonpublic Personal Information:
• Name, address, SSN
• Account balances
• Transaction history

Model privacy notice available

flashcards.front: What SECURITIES LICENSES are common?
flashcards.back:
FINRA Licenses (Series):

• SERIES 6: Investment company/variable products
• SERIES 7: General securities representative
• SERIES 63: Uniform Securities Agent (state law)
• SERIES 65: Investment adviser representative
• SERIES 66: Combined 63 and 65

SIE: Securities Industry Essentials (prerequisite)

Must be associated with firm to take

flashcards.front: What INSURANCE LICENSES are required?
flashcards.back:
Insurance Licensing:

State-Regulated:
• Each state has own requirements
• Must be licensed where selling

Common Lines:
• LIFE: Life insurance, annuities
• HEALTH: Health, disability, LTC
• PROPERTY & CASUALTY: Home, auto
• VARIABLE: For variable products (plus securities)

 CE required for renewal
Non-resident licenses available
flashcards.example: Term: $1M coverage, 20-year term, $50/month. Whole life: $500,000 coverage, $400/month, builds cash value.

flashcards.front: What are CFP® CONTINUING EDUCATION requirements?
flashcards.back:
CFP CE Requirements:

30 HOURS every 2 years:
• 2 hours CFP Board Ethics
• 28 hours various topics

Topics:
• Financial planning process
• Any principal knowledge topics
• Ethics (beyond 2-hour requirement)

Cannot carry forward hours
Report by certification period end

flashcards.front: What is SUITABILITY vs BEST INTEREST?
flashcards.back:
Standard Comparison:

SUITABILITY (Traditional BD):
• Reasonable basis
• Customer-specific
• Product must "fit"
• Less rigorous

BEST INTEREST (Reg BI/Fiduciary):
• In client
