flashcards.example: CFP professional managing $500,000 must recommend lowest-cost index funds if appropriate, not high-commission products.

flashcards.front: What are AML requirements for financial professionals?
flashcards.back:
AML (Anti-Money Laundering):

Bank Secrecy Act Requirements:
• Customer Identification Program (CIP)
• Know Your Customer (KYC)
• Suspicious Activity Reports (SARs)
• Currency Transaction Reports (CTRs)

CTR: Cash transactions >$10,000

AML compliance program required
Ongoing monitoring
Cannot "tip off" subject of SAR

flashcards.front: What are obligations for SENIOR/ELDER clients?
flashcards.back:
Senior Client Considerations:

• Cognitive decline awareness
• Trusted contact designation
• Exploitation/abuse concerns
• Communication adjustments
• Documentation emphasis

Regulations Allow:
• Temporary hold on disbursements
• Report suspected abuse
• Contact trusted person

NASSA and FINRA senior rules
Heightened vulnerability recognition
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What DOCUMENTATION should be maintained?
flashcards.back:
Documentation Requirements:

Client Records:
• Engagement agreements
• Data gathering forms
• Analysis and recommendations
• Meeting notes
• Communications
• Signed acknowledgments

Retention:
• Varies by regulation (typically 5-7 years)
• Beyond client relationship

Purpose: Compliance, protection, service quality

flashcards.front: What are ADVERTISING rules for financial professionals?
flashcards.back:
Advertising Standards:

Prohibited:
• False or misleading statements
• Guarantees of results
• Cherry-picked performance
• Testimonials without disclosure

Required:
• Balanced presentation
• Material fact disclosure
• Appropriate disclaimers
• Performance methodology

SEC Marketing Rule (2020) updates
Social media same standards

flashcards.front: What is the SEC MARKETING RULE?
flashcards.back:
SEC Marketing Rule (Nov 2022):

Replace prior advertising/testimonial rules

Now Permitted (with conditions):
• Testimonials
• Endorsements  
• Third-party ratings
• Performance advertising

Requirements:
• Disclosure of compensation
• Conflicts disclosed
• No misleading presentations
• Written agreement for promoters

flashcards.front: What are rules for REFERRAL arrangements?
flashcards.back:
Referral Fee Rules:

• Must disclose to client
• Cannot compromise best interest
• Written agreement recommended
• Fee reasonable

Cash vs Non-Cash:
• Cash: More scrutiny
• Non-cash: Still requires disclosure

Referral to:
• Attorneys, CPAs
• Other advisers
• Insurance agents

Document referral and compensation

flashcards.front: What are CUSTODY rules?
flashcards.back:
Custody Requirements (SEC):

"Custody" = Holding client funds/securities

Requirements:
• Qualified custodian
• Account statements (quarterly)
• Annual surprise examination
• Form ADV disclosure

Deemed Custody:
• Authority to withdraw
• Signatory on accounts
• Trustee

Safeguard: Protect client assets
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What is DISCRETIONARY vs NON-DISCRETIONARY authority?
flashcards.back:
Trading Authority:

DISCRETIONARY:
• Adviser decides trades
• No per-trade client approval
• Written authorization required
• Power of attorney

NON-DISCRETIONARY:
• Client approves each trade
• Adviser recommends
• Client executes

Different compliance requirements
Discretionary = More responsibility

flashcards.front: What CYBERSECURITY obligations exist?
flashcards.back:
Cybersecurity Requirements:

SEC Guidance:
• Written policies and procedures
• Risk assessment
• Access controls
• Data protection
• Incident response plan
• Vendor management

Client Obligations:
• Safeguard client data
• Notify of breaches
• Secure communications

Regular testing and training

flashcards.front: What OUTSIDE BUSINESS ACTIVITIES must be disclosed?
flashcards.back:
Outside Business Activities:

Must Disclose to employer:
• Other employment
• Business ownership
• Board positions
• Speaking/writing for compensation

Must Disclose to clients:
• If creates conflict
• If affects recommendations

FINRA: Written notice to broker-dealer
RIA: Policy-dependent

flashcards.front: What are rules on GIFTS and entertainment?
flashcards.back:
Gifts and Entertainment:

FINRA Rule:
• $100 gift limit per person/year
• Reasonable business entertainment
• Cannot be conditioned on business

General Principles:
• Cannot influence recommendations
• Disclose if material
• Document
• Firm policies may be stricter

Reciprocal: Giving and receiving

flashcards.front: What are SUPERVISION requirements?
flashcards.back:
Supervision Obligations:

Firm must:
• Establish supervisory system
• Designate supervisory personnel
• Written supervisory procedures
• Review employee activities
• Annual compliance review

Branch office:
• Periodic inspections
• Remote supervision procedures

Liability: Firm and supervisor for failures

flashcards.front: What is E&O insurance for financial professionals?
flashcards.back:
E&O (Errors & Omissions) Insurance:

Covers:
• Professional negligence
• Errors in advice
• Omissions
• Defense costs

Typical Claims:
• Unsuitable recommendations
• Failure to diversify
• Misrepresentation
• Breach of fiduciary duty

Not required but strongly recommended
Firm may provide or require
flashcards.example: CFP professional managing $500,000 must recommend lowest-cost index funds if appropriate, not high-commission products.

flashcards.front: How are client DISPUTES resolved?
flashcards.back:
Dispute Resolution:

FINRA ARBITRATION:
• Required for BD disputes
• Binding decision
• Panel of arbitrators
• Limited appeals

MEDIATION:
• Voluntary
• Facilitated negotiation
• Non-binding

LITIGATION:
• Court system
• More expensive/lengthy

Client agreements often mandate arbitration

flashcards.front: What does the SEC regulate?
flashcards.back:
SEC (Securities and Exchange Commission):

Regulates:
• Securities markets
• Investment advisers (>$100M AUM)
• Mutual funds
• Public companies
• Broker-dealers (through FINRA)

Key Rules:
• Securities Act of 1933
• Securities Exchange Act of 1934
• Investment Advisers Act of 1940
• Investment Company Act of 1940

flashcards.front: What do STATE REGULATORS oversee?
flashcards.back:
State Securities Regulators:

Oversee:
• Smaller investment advisers (<$100M)
• Investment adviser representatives
• State securities registration
• Insurance (separate department)

NASSA: North American Securities Administrators Association

Uniform Acts:
• Uniform Securities Act
• Blue Sky laws

Coordinate with federal regulators

flashcards.front: What rules apply to client COMMUNICATIONS?
flashcards.back:
Communication Standards:

• Fair and balanced
• Not misleading
• Substantiate claims
• Clear and prominent disclosures
• Appropriate for audience

Retention:
• Emails: 3+ years (SEC)
• Written: 6 years (FINRA)

Social Media:
• Same standards apply
• Pre-approval may be required
• Recordkeeping required

flashcards.front: What are obligations when TERMINATING engagement?
flashcards.back:
Engagement Termination:

Professional Obligations:
• Proper notice
• Transfer records appropriately
• Assist transition
• Return client property
• Final accounting

Continuing Duties:
• Confidentiality
• No disparagement
• Respond to regulatory inquiries

Document reasons and process

flashcards.front: What is PRODUCT DUE DILIGENCE?
flashcards.back:
Product Due Diligence:

Before Recommending:
• Understand product fully
• Evaluate risks and costs
• Compare alternatives
• Assess suitability
• Review issuer/sponsor

Ongoing:
• Monitor performance
• Reassess suitability
• Update recommendations

Document analysis and rationale
flashcards.example: Recommending aggressive growth fund to 75-year-old retiree seeking income would violate suitability standards.

flashcards.front: What is a COMPLIANCE program?
flashcards.back:
Compliance Program Elements:

• Written policies and procedures
• Designated Chief Compliance Officer
• Regular training
• Monitoring and testing
• Annual review
• Corrective actions
• Recordkeeping

SEC Rule 206(4)-7: RIAs must adopt
FINRA: Similar requirements for BDs

Culture of compliance

flashcards.front: What obligations exist for DIMINISHED CAPACITY?
flashcards.back:
Diminished Capacity:

Recognize Signs:
• Confusion, forgetfulness
• Unusual transactions
• Personality changes
• Vulnerability to exploitation

Actions:
• Document observations
• Contact trusted contact person
• Consider temporary hold
• Report suspected abuse
• May need to resign account

Balance autonomy with protection
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What are ROBO-ADVISER considerations?
flashcards.back:
Robo-Adviser Compliance:

Same Standards Apply:
• Fiduciary duty
• Suitability/best interest
• Disclosure requirements
• Conflicts management

Unique Issues:
• Algorithm governance
• Questionnaire adequacy
• Human oversight
• Rebalancing disclosure
• Limitations of advice

SEC/FINRA guidance applicable
flashcards.example: CFP professional managing $500,000 must recommend lowest-cost index funds if appropriate, not high-commission products.

flashcards.front: What is required for maintaining PROFESSIONAL competence?
flashcards.back:
Professional Competence:

Ongoing Requirements:
• Continuing education
• Stay current on regulations
• Market/product knowledge
• Technology proficiency

Resources:
• Professional associations
• Industry publications
• Regulatory updates
• Peer networking

CFP Board: 30 hours every 2 years
FINRA: Regulatory Element + Firm Element CE

flashcards.front: What is the CFP® CODE OF ETHICS?
flashcards.back:
CFP Code of Ethics Principles:

1. INTEGRITY: Honest and candid
2. OBJECTIVITY: Professional judgment
3. COMPETENCE: Knowledge and skill
4. FAIRNESS: Impartial and fair
5. CONFIDENTIALITY: Protect information
6. PROFESSIONALISM: Dignified conduct
7. DILIGENCE: Prompt and thorough
8. COMPLIANCE: Laws and regulations

flashcards.front: When is comprehensive FINANCIAL PLANNING required?
flashcards.back:
Financial Planning Requirement:

When:
• Client agrees to planning relationship
• Advice requires understanding of broader circumstances
• Reasonably expected by client

Elements:
• Follow 7-step process
• Apply Practice Standards
• Document thoroughly

Limited scope permitted when appropriate
Must still meet fiduciary duty
flashcards.example: CFP professional managing $500,000 must recommend lowest-cost index funds if appropriate, not high-commission products.

flashcards.front: What MATERIAL INFORMATION must be disclosed?
flashcards.back:
Material Information Disclosure:

Always Disclose:
• Compensation arrangements
• Conflicts of interest
• Disciplinary history
• Business relationships
• Ownership interests
• Material limitations

Test: Would reasonable client consider important?

Timing: Before or at engagement
Format: Written preferred

flashcards.front: What must be reported to CFP BOARD?
flashcards.back:
CFP Board Reporting:

Must Report within 30 days:
• Criminal charges/convictions
• Civil actions
• Regulatory investigations
• Customer complaints
• Bankruptcy
• Termination for cause
• License revocation

Failure to report = Separate violation
Background check at certification and renewal

flashcards.front: What conduct can result in CFP® DISCIPLINE?
flashcards.back:
Grounds for Discipline:

• Ethics violations
• Standards violations
• Criminal conduct
• Fraud or deceit
• Misrepresentation
• Unauthorized use of marks
• Failure to cooperate
• Conduct reflecting adversely

Sanctions:
• Private censure
• Public censure
• Suspension (up to 5 years)
• Permanent revocation

flashcards.front: What is INFORMED CONSENT?
flashcards.back:
Informed Consent:

Required for:
• Conflicts of interest
• Material limitations
• Significant risks
• Compensation arrangements

Elements:
• Full disclosure
• Client understanding
• Voluntary agreement
• Documented

Not one-time: Ongoing as circumstances change

flashcards.front: How should RISK TOLERANCE be assessed?
flashcards.back:
Risk Tolerance Assessment:

Components:
• RISK CAPACITY: Financial ability
• RISK ATTITUDE: Psychological comfort
• RISK PERCEPTION: Understanding of risk

Methods:
• Questionnaires
• Discussions
• Behavioral observations
• Scenario analysis

Document and revisit regularly
Reconcile conflicts between components
flashcards.example: Questionnaire scores: time horizon 10+ years (high), loss tolerance 15% (moderate), income stability high → moderate-aggressive allocation.

flashcards.front: What are obligations during IMPLEMENTATION?
flashcards.back:
Implementation Duties:

• Execute recommendations promptly
• Use appropriate products
• Consider tax consequences
• Minimize transaction costs
• Coordinate with other advisers
• Document actions taken
• Confirm with client

If client doesn

flashcards.front: What are MONITORING obligations?
flashcards.back:
Monitoring Requirements:

If ongoing relationship:
• Regular reviews
• Update for life changes
• Reassess recommendations
• Rebalance as needed
• Communicate changes

Frequency: At least annually

No ongoing relationship:
• No monitoring duty
• But document scope

Triggers: Material life events

flashcards.front: What are STANDARDS OF PROFESSIONAL CONDUCT?
flashcards.back:
CFP Standards of Conduct Components:

1. CODE OF ETHICS
   • 8 principles

2. STANDARDS OF CONDUCT
   • Duties owed to clients
   • Duties owed to firms
   • Duties owed to CFP Board

3. PRACTICE STANDARDS
   • For financial planning
   • 7-step process

Effective: June 30, 2019
Apply to all CFP® professionals

flashcards.front: Common Behavioral Finance Biases
flashcards.back:
B - Bandwagon effect (herding)
I - Illusion of control
A - Anchoring (first number fixation)
S - Status quo bias

T - Transparency illusion
R - Recency bias
A - Availability heuristic
P - Present bias (hyperbolic discounting)
S - Sunk cost fallacy

flashcards.front: Effective Client Communication Techniques
flashcards.back:
A - Ask open-ended questions
C - Clarify and confirm understanding
T - Take notes (with permission)
I - Identify non-verbal cues
V - Validate feelings
E - Empathize without judgment

Paraphrase to show understanding
Summarize key points

flashcards.front: SMART Goal Framework
flashcards.back:
S - Specific (clear and defined)
M - Measurable (quantifiable)
A - Achievable (realistic)
R - Relevant (aligned with values)
T - Time-bound (deadline)

Example: "Save $10,000 for emergency fund in 18 months by auto-transferring $556/month"
flashcards.example: Save $10,000 for emergency fund in 18 months by auto-transferring $556/month

flashcards.front: What is LOSS AVERSION and how strong is its effect?
flashcards.back:
Loss aversion is the tendency to prefer avoiding losses over acquiring equivalent gains.

• Losses feel ~2x as painful as equivalent gains feel good (Kahneman/Tversky)
• Leads to holding losers too long, selling winners too early
• Drives irrational risk-seeking to avoid realizing losses

flashcards.front: What is the DISPOSITION EFFECT?
flashcards.back:
The tendency to sell winning investments too quickly and hold losing investments too long.

Causes:
• Loss aversion (don

flashcards.front: What is HERD MENTALITY (Herding)?
flashcards.back:
The tendency to follow and copy what others are doing, rather than making independent decisions.

Examples:
• Buying hot stocks or sectors everyone is buying
• Panic selling during market downturns
• Following "guru" advice without analysis
• Chasing trending investments

Creates bubbles and exacerbates crashes.

flashcards.front: What is STATUS QUO BIAS?
flashcards.back:
The preference for the current state of affairs, leading to resistance to change.

Examples:
• Staying in underperforming investments
• Not rebalancing portfolio
• Keeping outdated beneficiary designations
• Sticking with same advisor despite poor service
• Default 401(k) allocation never changed

flashcards.front: What are the FOUR MONEY SCRIPTS (Klontz)?
flashcards.back:
1. MONEY AVOIDANCE: Money is bad, wealthy are greedy

2. MONEY WORSHIP: More money solves all problems

3. MONEY STATUS: Self-worth = net worth

4. MONEY VIGILANCE: Save, be frugal, keep finances private

All except Vigilance correlate with financial problems.

flashcards.front: What characterizes MONEY AVOIDANCE?
flashcards.back:
Beliefs that money is bad or that wealthy people are greedy or corrupt.

Behaviors:
• Self-sabotaging financial success
• Giving away money inappropriately
• Avoiding thinking about finances
• Ignoring financial statements
• Guilt about having money

Often stems from religious beliefs or family messaging.

flashcards.front: What characterizes MONEY WORSHIP?
flashcards.back:
Belief that more money will solve all problems and bring happiness.

Behaviors:
• Workaholism at expense of relationships
• Compulsive buying/overspending
• Secret spending or hoarding
• Unrealistic expectations about wealth
• Never feeling "enough"

Linked to lower income and net worth.

flashcards.front: What characterizes MONEY STATUS?
flashcards.back:
Belief that self-worth is tied to net worth; using money to impress others.

Behaviors:
• Overspending to appear wealthy
• Competitive with peers about possessions
• Lying about income or assets
• Financial infidelity in relationships
• Credit card overuse

Linked to gambling, overspending, financial dependence.

flashcards.front: What characterizes MONEY VIGILANCE?
flashcards.back:
Beliefs that money should be saved, not discussed, and frugality is virtuous.

Behaviors:
• High savings rate
• Reluctance to discuss finances
• Anxiety about spending
• Difficulty enjoying money
• May be overly secretive

Most positively associated with financial health, though excessive vigilance can cause anxiety.

flashcards.front: What is ACTIVE LISTENING? (SOLAR method)
flashcards.back:
Active listening involves fully concentrating on what is being said.

SOLAR method:
• S - Square (face client directly)
• O - Open (keep open body posture)
• L - Lean (lean forward slightly)
• A - Acknowledge (eye contact)
• R - Relax (stay calm and present)

Also: paraphrase, summarize, ask clarifying questions.

flashcards.front: What are OPEN-ENDED vs CLOSED-ENDED questions?
flashcards.back:
OPEN-ENDED: Cannot be answered with yes/no; encourage elaboration.
• "What are your retirement goals?"
• "How do you feel about market volatility?"
• "Tell me about your investment experience"

CLOSED-ENDED: Yes/no or short specific answers.
• "Do you have a will?"
• "When do you want to retire?"

Use open-ended to build rapport and gather information.
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What is MOTIVATIONAL INTERVIEWING?
flashcards.back:
A client-centered counseling approach to elicit behavior change by helping clients explore and resolve ambivalence.

Key principles (OARS):
• Open questions
• Affirmations
• Reflective listening
• Summarizing

Used to help clients make changes like saving more, spending less, or following through on plans.

flashcards.front: What is REFLECTIVE LISTENING?
flashcards.back:
Repeating or paraphrasing what the client said to confirm understanding and show engagement.

Types:
• Simple: Repeat almost exactly
• Paraphrase: Restate in your own words
• Feeling: Reflect the emotion ("It sounds like you

flashcards.front: What are signs of CLIENT RESISTANCE?
flashcards.back:
Signs that a client is resistant to advice or recommendations:

• Arguing or interrupting
• Discounting ("That won

flashcards.front: What is TRANSFERENCE in the planner-client relationship?
flashcards.back:
Transference: Client unconsciously redirects feelings about a person from their past onto the planner.

Examples:
• Client sees planner as "authority figure" like parent
• Inappropriate anger, affection, or dependence
• Expecting planner to "fix everything"

Recognize and maintain professional boundaries; may need referral.

flashcards.front: What is COUNTERTRANSFERENCE?
flashcards.back:
Countertransference: Planner unconsciously redirects feelings onto the client.

Examples:
• Becoming emotionally involved with client

flashcards.front: What are the STAGES OF CHANGE (Prochaska)?
flashcards.back:
1. PRECONTEMPLATION: Not thinking about change

2. CONTEMPLATION: Aware of need, weighing pros/cons

3. PREPARATION: Intending to act, making plans

4. ACTION: Actively modifying behavior

5. MAINTENANCE: Sustaining new behavior

6. TERMINATION: New behavior is permanent

Tailor advice to client

flashcards.front: When should a CFP refer client to a mental health professional?
flashcards.back:
Refer when issues are beyond financial planning scope:

• Signs of depression or anxiety disorders
• Compulsive behaviors (gambling, shopping)
• Grief interfering with decisions
• Addiction issues
• Family conflict requiring mediation
• Trauma affecting financial behavior
• Signs of cognitive decline
• Suicidal ideation (emergency)

flashcards.front: What is FINANCIAL THERAPY?
flashcards.back:
Financial therapy integrates cognitive, emotional, behavioral, and relational aspects of financial well-being.

• Combines financial planning with therapeutic techniques
• Addresses emotional and psychological barriers to financial health
• Not within scope of CFP practice alone
• CFP-certified financial therapist requires additional training
• Often involves collaboration with mental health professionals

flashcards.front: How do LIFE EVENTS affect financial planning psychology?
flashcards.back:
Major life events create emotional and cognitive challenges:

• Marriage/divorce: Power dynamics, conflicting values
• Birth of child: Protection instinct, overwhelm
• Death of loved one: Grief, poor decisions
• Job loss: Identity crisis, anxiety
• Retirement: Loss of purpose, fear
• Inheritance: Guilt, overwhelm, lifestyle inflation
• Health crises: Fear, denial

Allow time, provide extra support during transitions.
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What is FINANCIAL INFIDELITY?
flashcards.back:
Secretly spending money, hiding accounts/debts, or lying to partner about finances.

Signs:
• Hidden credit cards or accounts
• Unexplained purchases
• Defensive about money discussions
• Missing money from joint accounts

Planner considerations:
• Maintain confidentiality per engagement
• Encourage honest communication
• May affect planning assumptions
• Consider separate meetings if needed

flashcards.front: How do POWER DYNAMICS affect couples\
flashcards.back:
Power imbalances can affect decision-making and satisfaction:

Sources of power:
• Income disparity
• Financial knowledge
• Control of accounts
• Cultural/family expectations
• Personality dominance

Planner strategies:
• Meet with each partner separately
• Ensure both voices are heard
• Watch for non-verbal cues
• Address disagreements constructively
• Avoid taking sides

flashcards.front: What is COGNITIVE DECLINE and how should planners respond?
flashcards.back:
Signs of potential cognitive decline:
• Repeated questions or forgetting conversations
• Difficulty with calculations or concepts
• Unusual financial decisions
• Confusion about accounts or investments
• Vulnerability to scams

Planner response:
• Document observations
• Involve trusted contacts if designated
• Avoid complex decisions during decline
• Know elder abuse reporting requirements
• Consider referral for evaluation
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: How do CULTURAL DIFFERENCES affect financial planning?
flashcards.back:
Cultural factors shape financial values and behaviors:

• Family obligations (supporting parents, extended family)
• Decision-making processes (individual vs collective)
• Gender roles and money control
• Attitudes toward debt
• Views on inheritance and wealth transfer
• Comfort with financial discussions
• Trust in institutions and professionals

Be curious, avoid assumptions, ask about preferences.
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What is SYSTEM 1 vs SYSTEM 2 thinking (Kahneman)?
flashcards.back:
SYSTEM 1 (Fast):
• Automatic, intuitive
• Emotional, instinctive
• Little effort, always "on"
• Prone to biases
• Example: Panic selling in a crash

SYSTEM 2 (Slow):
• Deliberate, analytical
• Logical, effortful
• Lazy, activated when needed
• Better for complex decisions
• Example: Calculating retirement needs

Most financial decisions benefit from System 2.
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What are common HEURISTICS in financial decisions?
flashcards.back:
Heuristics = mental shortcuts that can lead to errors:

• AVAILABILITY: Judge likelihood by ease of recall
• REPRESENTATIVENESS: Judge by similarity to stereotype
• ANCHORING: Over-rely on first information received
• AFFECT: Decide based on current emotions
• RECOGNITION: Prefer familiar options

Useful for quick decisions but can lead to systematic errors.

flashcards.front: What is CHOICE OVERLOAD?
flashcards.back:
Having too many options can lead to:

• Decision paralysis (no decision made)
• Lower satisfaction with choice made
• Regret about options not chosen
• Default to status quo

Example: 401(k) with 50 fund options → lower participation

Solutions:
• Limit options presented
• Provide defaults
• Categorize choices
• Make recommendations

flashcards.front: What is NUDGE THEORY (Thaler & Sunstein)?
flashcards.back:
Nudges are small design changes that influence behavior without restricting choice.

Examples:
• Auto-enrollment in 401(k) plans
• Auto-escalation of contribution rates
• Default investment in target-date fund
• Making savings salient and easy

"Libertarian paternalism" - guide toward better outcomes while preserving freedom.

flashcards.front: What is REGRET AVERSION?
flashcards.back:
Fear of making a decision that turns out to be wrong leads to:

• Inaction (to avoid responsibility for bad outcome)
• Following the crowd (shared blame)
• Avoiding risky investments
• Analysis paralysis
• Hindsight bias ("I knew it would happen")

Counter by:
• Focusing on process, not outcome
• Documenting decision rationale
• Diversifying (reduces regret potential)

flashcards.front: What is FINANCIAL WELL-BEING (CFPB definition)?
flashcards.back:
CFPB defines financial well-being as having:

1. Control over day-to-day finances
2. Capacity to absorb financial shocks
3. On track to meet financial goals
4. Freedom to make choices that allow enjoyment of life

Not just about money—includes security, freedom, and ability to pursue goals.

flashcards.front: How does FINANCIAL STRESS affect people?
flashcards.back:
Physical effects:
• Sleep problems
• Headaches, muscle tension
• High blood pressure
• Weakened immune system

Mental effects:
• Anxiety and depression
• Relationship conflict
• Poor work performance
• Avoidance behaviors

Planners should recognize stress signs and address emotional component of planning.

flashcards.front: What is MASLOW\
flashcards.back: Financial needs parallel Maslow

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between MONEY and HAPPINESS?
flashcards.back:
Research findings:

• Money increases happiness up to a point (~$75-100K for emotional well-being)
• Above that, life satisfaction continues to rise but slowly
• Spending on experiences > spending on things
• Spending on others increases happiness
• Financial security reduces stress/anxiety
• Autonomy and purpose matter more than income level
• Comparison to peers affects satisfaction

flashcards.front: What is SCARCITY MINDSET vs ABUNDANCE MINDSET?
flashcards.back:
SCARCITY MINDSET:
• Fear-based decisions
• Focus on what might be lost
• Hoarding behavior
• Tunnel vision on immediate needs
• Difficulty planning long-term

ABUNDANCE MINDSET:
• Opportunity-focused
• Belief in sufficient resources
• Generous, growth-oriented
• Long-term perspective
• Open to calculated risks

Scarcity often comes from past experiences.

flashcards.front: What are the 401(k) contribution limits for 2026?
flashcards.back:
2026 Limits:
• Employee deferral: $24,500
• Catch-up (50+): $7,500 additional
• Super catch-up (60-63): $11,250 (SECURE 2.0)
• Total (with employer): $71,500
• Catch-up total: $79,000

Note: Limits adjust annually for inflation

flashcards.front: What is a SAFE HARBOR 401(k)?
flashcards.back:
Safe Harbor 401(k):
• Automatically passes ADP/ACP testing
• Required employer contribution:
  - 3% non-elective, OR
  - 4% match (100% of first 3%, 50% of next 2%)
• Immediately 100% vested
• Lower admin burden

flashcards.front: What is the ADP/ACP test for 401(k) plans?
flashcards.back:
ADP Test (Actual Deferral Percentage):
• Tests employee deferrals

ACP Test (Actual Contribution Percentage):
• Tests employer matching/after-tax

HCEs cannot exceed NHCEs by:
• More than 2% absolute, OR
• More than 2x (if NHCE ≤2%)

flashcards.front: What is the difference between TRADITIONAL and ROTH IRA?
flashcards.back:
TRADITIONAL:
• Deductible contributions (income limits if covered by plan)
• Tax-deferred growth
• Taxable withdrawals
• RMDs at 73

ROTH:
• Non-deductible contributions
• Tax-free growth
• Tax-free qualified withdrawals
• No RMDs for owner
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: What are the IRA contribution limits for 2026?
flashcards.back:
2026 IRA Limits:
• Contribution: $7,500
• Catch-up (50+): $1,000 additional
• Total if 50+: $8,500

Must have earned income
Can contribute to both traditional and Roth (combined limit)
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: What are ROTH IRA income limits for 2026?
flashcards.back:
2026 Roth IRA Phaseouts (MAGI):

Single:
• Full: <$154,000
• Partial: $154,000-$169,000
• None: >$169,000

Married Filing Jointly:
• Full: <$240,000
• Partial: $240,000-$250,000
• None: >$250,000
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: What is a BACKDOOR ROTH IRA?
flashcards.back:
Backdoor Roth Strategy:
1. Contribute to non-deductible Traditional IRA
2. Convert to Roth IRA
3. Pay tax only on any gains

Warning: Pro-rata rule applies if you have other traditional IRA balances

Used when income exceeds Roth limits
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: What is the PRO-RATA rule for IRA conversions?
flashcards.back:
Pro-Rata Rule:
• ALL traditional IRAs aggregated
• Cannot convert only non-deductible portion

Formula:
Taxable % = Pre-tax balance ÷ Total IRA balance

Solution: Roll pre-tax to 401(k) first
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: What is the 10% early withdrawal penalty exception list?
flashcards.back:
10% Penalty Exceptions:
• Death
• Disability
• Medical expenses >7.5% AGI
• Health insurance (if unemployed)
• SEPP (72t payments)
• First home ($10K IRA only)
• Higher education (IRA only)
• Birth/adoption ($5K)
• Age 55 separation from service (401k)
flashcards.example: Client contributes $23,000 (2024 limit) plus $7,500 catch-up (age 50+). Employer matches 50% up to 6% of salary.

flashcards.front: What are REQUIRED MINIMUM DISTRIBUTIONS (RMDs)?
flashcards.back:
RMDs:
• Begin at age 73 (SECURE 2.0)
• Based on Uniform Lifetime Table
• Must take by Dec 31 each year
• 1st year: Can defer to April 1 next year
• 25% penalty for missed RMDs

RMD = Account Balance ÷ Life Expectancy Factor
flashcards.example: IRA balance $500,000, age 73, life expectancy factor 26.5: RMD = $500,000 / 26.5 = $18,868 annual distribution.

flashcards.front: What is a QUALIFIED CHARITABLE DISTRIBUTION (QCD)?
flashcards.back:
QCD Features:
• Direct IRA transfer to charity
• Age 70½ or older
• Up to $100K/year
• Satisfies RMD requirement
• Excluded from taxable income
• Must be to qualified charity
• Not from SEP/SIMPLE IRAs if active
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: What is FULL RETIREMENT AGE for Social Security?
flashcards.back:
Full Retirement Age (FRA):
• Born 1943-1954: Age 66
• Born 1955: 66 + 2 months
• Born 1956: 66 + 4 months
• Born 1957: 66 + 6 months
• Born 1958: 66 + 8 months
• Born 1959: 66 + 10 months
• Born 1960+: Age 67
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What are SOCIAL SECURITY early and delayed retirement credits?
flashcards.back:
Early Claiming (before FRA):
• Reduction of 5/9% per month for first 36 months
• 5/12% per month beyond 36 months
• At 62: ~25-30% reduction

Delayed Credits (after FRA):
• 8% per year increase
• Maximum at age 70
• 24-32% increase if delay to 70
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What is the SOCIAL SECURITY earnings test?
flashcards.back:
Earnings Test (before FRA):
• 2024 limit: $22,320/year
• Lose $1 for every $2 over limit

Year of FRA:
• Higher limit: $59,520
• Lose $1 for every $3 over

After FRA: No reduction
Note: Lost benefits restored over time
flashcards.example: Full retirement age 67, monthly benefit $2,500. Claiming at 62: $2,500 × 0.7 = $1,750/month (30% reduction).

flashcards.front: How is a SPOUSE\
flashcards.back: Spousal Benefit:\n• Up to 50% of worker

flashcards.front: How is the SURVIVOR\
flashcards.back: Survivor Benefits:\n• 100% of deceased

flashcards.front: What is a DEFINED BENEFIT plan?
flashcards.back:
Defined Benefit Plan:
• Employer funds entirely
• Promise specific retirement benefit
• Formula: Years × Salary × %
• Employer bears investment risk
• PBGC insurance protection
• Vesting: 5 year cliff or 3-7 graded
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What is the PENSION BENEFIT GUARANTY CORPORATION (PBGC)?
flashcards.back:
PBGC:
• Federal agency
• Insures DB plan benefits
• Steps in if plan fails
• Does NOT cover DC plans
• Maximum guarantee limit (~$80K/year at 65)
• Funded by employer premiums
• Single and multiemployer plans

flashcards.front: What is a PROFIT SHARING plan?
flashcards.back:
Profit Sharing Plan:
• Defined contribution plan
• Employer contributes discretionary amount
• Up to 25% of eligible compensation
• Can skip contributions
• Allocation formulas vary
• Popular for small business

flashcards.front: What is a SEP-IRA and its contribution limits?
flashcards.back:
SEP-IRA (Simplified Employee Pension):
• Employer contributions only
• Up to 25% of compensation
• Max: $71,500 (2026)
• Self-employed: ~20% of net SE income
• Easy to establish/maintain
• Must cover eligible employees
• Deadline: Tax filing + extensions
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: What is a SIMPLE IRA?
flashcards.back:
SIMPLE IRA:
• For employers <100 employees
• Employee deferrals: $17,000 (2026)
• Catch-up (50+): $3,500
• Required employer match:
  - 3% match, OR
  - 2% non-elective
• 25% penalty for early withdrawal in first 2 years
• No other qualified plans allowed
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: What is a SOLO 401(k)?
flashcards.back:
Solo 401(k) (Individual 401k):
• Self-employed, no employees
• Employee + employer contributions
• 2026 limit: $71,500 total
• Catch-up: $7,500 additional
• Roth option available
• Loans permitted
• Higher contributions than SEP
flashcards.example: Client contributes $23,000 (2024 limit) plus $7,500 catch-up (age 50+). Employer matches 50% up to 6% of salary.

flashcards.front: What is a MONEY PURCHASE PENSION plan?
flashcards.back:
Money Purchase Pension:
• Defined contribution plan
• MANDATORY annual employer contribution
• Fixed percentage of compensation
• Up to 25% of eligible comp
• Less flexible than profit sharing
• Less common today (merged with PS limits)

flashcards.front: What is a 403(b) plan?
flashcards.back:
403(b) Tax-Sheltered Annuity:
• For public schools, 501(c)(3) organizations
• Same limits as 401(k): $24,500 (2026)
• 15-year rule: Extra $3,000/year
• Investments: Annuities or mutual funds
• May have church plan features
• Catch-up: $7,500 (50+), $11,250 (60-63)

flashcards.front: What is a 457(b) plan?
flashcards.back:
457(b) Deferred Compensation:
• State/local government, tax-exempt orgs
• Deferral limit: $24,500 (2026)
• "Double limit" catch-up in 3 years before retirement
• NO 10% early withdrawal penalty
• Separate from 401(k)/403(b) limits
• Catch-up: $7,500 (50+)
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What are VESTING schedules for employer contributions?
flashcards.back:
Vesting Options:

CLIFF VESTING:
• 0% until requirement met
• 100% after (3 years DC, 5 years DB)

GRADED VESTING:
• Gradual increase
• 2-6 years for DC plans
• 3-7 years for DB plans

Employee contributions: Always 100% vested

flashcards.front: What is a HARDSHIP WITHDRAWAL from 401(k)?
flashcards.back:
Hardship Withdrawal:
• Immediate and heavy financial need
• No other resources available
• Allowed reasons:
  - Medical expenses
  - Home purchase
  - Tuition
  - Prevent eviction
  - Funeral expenses
• Subject to income tax
• 10% penalty may apply
• Cannot borrow amount

flashcards.front: What are the 401(k) LOAN rules?
flashcards.back:
401(k) Loan Rules:
• Up to 50% of vested balance
• Maximum: $50,000
• Repay within 5 years (15 for home)
• Interest paid to yourself
• If terminate: Full balance due
• Defaulted loan = distribution + penalty
• Double taxation on interest

flashcards.front: What is the difference between DIRECT and INDIRECT rollover?
flashcards.back:
DIRECT ROLLOVER (Trustee-to-Trustee):
• Funds go directly between custodians
• No withholding
• No 60-day limit
• Preferred method

INDIRECT ROLLOVER:
• You receive check
• 20% mandatory withholding
• Must redeposit within 60 days
• Once per 12 months (IRA)
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What are the ROLLOVER OPTIONS when leaving a job?
flashcards.back:
Options for 401(k) at Job Change:
1. Leave in old plan (if allowed)
2. Roll to new employer 401(k)
3. Roll to Traditional IRA
4. Roll to Roth IRA (taxable conversion)
5. Cash out (taxes + penalty)

Consider: fees, investment options, creditor protection
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: How to calculate RETIREMENT INCOME NEED?
flashcards.back:
Income Replacement Method:

1. Estimate pre-retirement income
2. Apply replacement ratio (70-80%)
3. Subtract Social Security
4. Subtract pension income
5. Remaining = Required from savings

Real dollar adjustment for inflation needed
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What is the 4% WITHDRAWAL RULE?
flashcards.back:
4% Rule (Bengen):
• Withdraw 4% of portfolio in year 1
• Adjust for inflation each year
• 30-year retirement horizon
• Based on ~50/50 stock/bond mix

Limitations:
• Based on historical data
• May need adjustment for:
  - Low rates
  - Longer retirement
  - Market conditions
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: How to calculate CAPITAL PRESERVATION vs CAPITAL UTILIZATION?
flashcards.back:
CAPITAL PRESERVATION:
• Never touch principal
• Live only on income/growth
• Use PV of perpetuity formula

CAPITAL UTILIZATION:
• Spend principal over time
• Target zero at end of plan
• Use PV of annuity formula
• More aggressive withdrawal rate

flashcards.front: What are the PARTS OF MEDICARE?
flashcards.back:
Medicare Parts:
• Part A: Hospital insurance (free if worked 10+ years)
• Part B: Medical insurance ($185/month 2026)
• Part C: Medicare Advantage (private)
• Part D: Prescription drugs (varies)

Original Medicare = A + B
Medigap supplements A & B

flashcards.front: When should you ENROLL in Medicare?
flashcards.back:
Medicare Enrollment:
• Initial: 3 months before to 3 months after 65th birthday
• Late enrollment: penalty

Exceptions:
• Working with employer coverage
• Special Enrollment Period when work ends
• 8 months after employment ends

Part B penalty: 10% per year late

flashcards.front: What are IRMAA surcharges for Medicare?
flashcards.back:
IRMAA (Income-Related Monthly Adjustment):
• Higher premiums for high earners
• Affects Part B and Part D
• Based on MAGI from 2 years ago
• 2024 thresholds:
  - Single: >$103,000
  - MFJ: >$206,000
• Strategy: Manage income in pre-Medicare years

flashcards.front: How can HSAs be used in RETIREMENT?
flashcards.back:
HSA Retirement Strategy:
• After 65: No penalty for non-medical (just income tax)
• Before 65: Tax-free for qualified medical only
• Can pay Medicare premiums (not Medigap)
• Reimburse past medical expenses anytime
• Carry over balance indefinitely
• Superior to Traditional IRA for medical
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What is SEQUENCE OF RETURNS risk?
flashcards.back:
Sequence Risk:
• Order of returns matters when withdrawing
• Early negative returns + withdrawals = devastating
• Can

flashcards.front: What is a BUCKET STRATEGY for retirement?
flashcards.back:
Bucket Strategy:
• Bucket 1 (1-2 years): Cash/short-term
• Bucket 2 (3-10 years): Bonds/moderate
• Bucket 3 (10+ years): Stocks/growth

Benefits:
• Reduces sequence risk
• Psychological comfort
• Avoid selling equities in downturns
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What is tax-efficient WITHDRAWAL SEQUENCING?
flashcards.back:
Withdrawal Order Strategy:

Traditional approach:
1. Taxable accounts first
2. Tax-deferred second
3. Roth last

Optimal approach:
• Fill lower tax brackets from tax-deferred
• Use Roth to avoid higher brackets
• Consider capital gains rates

flashcards.front: What are key SECURE 2.0 Act provisions?
flashcards.back:
SECURE 2.0 (2022):
• RMD age: 73 (2023), 75 (2033)
• Catch-up Roth for high earners
• 529 to Roth rollover (limits apply)
• Reduced RMD penalty (25%/10%)
• Employer match can be Roth
• Emergency savings accounts
• Student loan matching allowed

flashcards.front: What is the 10-YEAR RULE for inherited IRAs?
flashcards.back:
10-Year Rule (SECURE Act):
• Most non-spouse beneficiaries
• Must empty account within 10 years
• No annual RMD requirements (but may be)
• Exceptions (Eligible Designated Beneficiaries):
  - Spouse
  - Minor children (until majority)
  - Disabled/chronically ill
  - Not more than 10 years younger
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: What are SPOUSE options for inherited retirement accounts?
flashcards.back:
Spouse Inherited Account Options:

1. ROLL TO OWN IRA:
   • Treat as own
   • RMDs at own age 73

2. INHERITED IRA:
   • Use own life expectancy
   • Can withdraw before 59½ without penalty

3. LUMP SUM:
   • Fully taxable
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What is NET UNREALIZED APPRECIATION (NUA)?
flashcards.back:
NUA Strategy:
• Applies to employer stock in 401(k)
• Lump sum distribution to taxable account
• Pay ordinary income tax on cost basis only
• NUA taxed as LTCG when sold

Best when:
• Large appreciation
• Low cost basis
• Lower tax bracket at distribution

flashcards.front: What is a SUBSTANTIALLY EQUAL PERIODIC PAYMENT (72t)?
flashcards.back:
72(t) SEPP Rules:
• Avoid 10% early withdrawal penalty
• Must continue for 5 years OR until 59½
• Three calculation methods:
  - Required Minimum Distribution
  - Fixed Amortization
  - Fixed Annuitization
• Cannot modify once started
• Penalty if broken: retroactive
flashcards.example: IRA balance $500,000, age 73, life expectancy factor 26.5: RMD = $500,000 / 26.5 = $18,868 annual distribution.

flashcards.front: What is an IMMEDIATE ANNUITY?
flashcards.back:
Immediate Annuity (SPIA):
• Lump sum premium
• Payments begin immediately
• Fixed or variable payments
• Life, period certain, or both
• No accumulation phase
• Addresses longevity risk
• Irrevocable (usually)

flashcards.front: What is a DEFERRED ANNUITY?
flashcards.back:
Deferred Annuity:
• Accumulation phase before payments
• Fixed, variable, or indexed
• Tax-deferred growth
• Surrender charges apply
• Annuitization optional
• Death benefit features
• Income riders available

flashcards.front: How are NON-QUALIFIED annuities taxed?
flashcards.back:
Non-Qualified Annuity Taxation:

During Accumulation:
• Tax-deferred growth

Withdrawals (before annuitization):
• LIFO - earnings first (taxable)
• 10% penalty before 59½

Annuity Payments:
• Exclusion ratio (return of basis tax-free)
• Remaining = taxable

flashcards.front: What retirement planning considerations for LONG-TERM CARE?
flashcards.back:
LTC Retirement Planning:
• 70% chance of needing some LTC after 65
• Medicare: Only 100 days skilled nursing
• Medicaid: Must "spend down" assets
• LTC Insurance: Self-insure risk
• Hybrid products: Life + LTC
• Average nursing home: $7,000-10,000/month
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: How to calculate RETIREMENT GAP?
flashcards.back: Retirement Gap Calculation:\n\n1. Desired annual income (today
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What is the WINDFALL ELIMINATION PROVISION (WEP)?
flashcards.back:
WEP:
• Reduces Social Security for those with pension from non-covered employment
• Affects government workers not paying SS tax
• Modified formula reduces benefit
• Maximum reduction ~$500/month
• Eliminated if 30+ years substantial earnings
flashcards.example: Full retirement age 67, monthly benefit $2,500. Claiming at 62: $2,500 × 0.7 = $1,750/month (30% reduction).

flashcards.front: What is the GOVERNMENT PENSION OFFSET (GPO)?
flashcards.back:
GPO:
• Affects spousal/survivor SS benefits
• If receiving pension from non-covered employment
• Offset = 2/3 of government pension
• Can reduce SS spousal benefit to zero
• Different from WEP
• Applies to government workers

flashcards.front: What is a CROSS-TESTED or AGE-WEIGHTED plan?
flashcards.back:
Cross-Tested Plan:
• Profit sharing allocation method
• Benefits tested, not contributions
• Favors older employees
• Must pass nondiscrimination testing
• Good for older business owners
• Younger employees get less

Age-Weighted:
• Similar, uses age factor in allocation

flashcards.front: What is a CASH BALANCE plan?
flashcards.back:
Cash Balance Plan:
• Hybrid: DB promise + DC presentation
• Hypothetical account balance
• Guaranteed return (pay credits)
• Portable like DC plan
• Favors older employees less than DB
• PBGC covered
• Popular for small to mid-size firms

flashcards.front: What is a ROTH CONVERSION LADDER?
flashcards.back:
Roth Conversion Ladder:
• Early retirement strategy
• Convert Traditional to Roth each year
• Pay tax on conversion
• Wait 5 years to access converted amount
• Fill low tax brackets
• Avoid 10% penalty
• Requires planning 5+ years ahead
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What is REPLACEMENT RATIO and typical targets?
flashcards.back:
Replacement Ratio:
• Retirement income ÷ Pre-retirement income

Targets:
• General rule: 70-80%
• Higher income: May need less %
• Lower income: May need higher %

Factors reducing need:
• No payroll taxes
• No retirement savings
• Paid-off mortgage
• Lower expenses
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What are the MAJOR RETIREMENT RISKS?
flashcards.back:
Retirement Risks:
• Longevity: Outliving money
• Inflation: Purchasing power erosion
• Sequence of Returns: Early losses
• Healthcare: Rising medical costs
• Market: Investment volatility
• Interest Rate: Income reduction
• Policy: Tax/benefit changes
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What is SOCIAL SECURITY OPTIMIZATION?
flashcards.back:
SS Optimization Strategies:
• Delay to maximize benefit
• Coordinate spousal claims
• Higher earner delays longer
• Consider break-even analysis
• Factor health and longevity
• Survivor benefit planning
• Minimize taxation of benefits
flashcards.example: Full retirement age 67, monthly benefit $2,500. Claiming at 62: $2,500 × 0.7 = $1,750/month (30% reduction).

flashcards.front: How is SOCIAL SECURITY taxed?
flashcards.back:
SS Taxation:
• Up to 85% can be taxable

Provisional Income = AGI + ½ SS + Tax-exempt interest

Single:
• <$25K: 0% taxable
• $25K-$34K: up to 50%
• >$34K: up to 85%

MFJ:
• <$32K: 0%
• $32K-$44K: up to 50%
• >$44K: up to 85%
flashcards.example: Full retirement age 67, monthly benefit $2,500. Claiming at 62: $2,500 × 0.7 = $1,750/month (30% reduction).

flashcards.front: What is AUTOMATIC ENROLLMENT in 401(k)?
flashcards.back:
Automatic Enrollment:
• Employees enrolled unless opt-out
• Default contribution rate (often 3%)
• Default investment (usually TDF)
• Safe harbor from fiduciary liability
• QACA: Qualified Automatic Contribution Arrangement
• EACA: Eligible Automatic Contribution Arrangement
• Increases participation significantly
flashcards.example: CFP professional managing $500,000 must recommend lowest-cost index funds if appropriate, not high-commission products.

flashcards.front: SECURE 2.0 Act Key Age Changes
flashcards.back:
RMD ages phased increase:
• Born 1950 or earlier: 72 (old rule)
• Born 1951-1959: 73 (2023+)
• Born 1960+: 75 (2033+)

Catch-up contributions:
• 50+: Standard catch-up
• 60-63: SUPER catch-up ($10,000 / 150%)

Penalty-free withdrawals:
• 59½: General rule
• 55: Rule of 55 (separation from service)

flashcards.front: Substantially Equal Periodic Payments - 72(t)
flashcards.back:
Three IRS-approved methods:

1. Required Minimum Distribution method
   (recalculates annually)

2. Amortization method
   (fixed payments)

3. Annuitization method
   (mortality table based)

Must continue 5 years OR until 59½ (whichever is LONGER)
flashcards.example: IRA balance $500,000, age 73, life expectancy factor 26.5: RMD = $500,000 / 26.5 = $18,868 annual distribution.

flashcards.front: SIMPLE IRA Key Requirements
flashcards.back:
Savings Incentive Match Plan for Employees:

• 100 or fewer employees
• $5,000 compensation in any 2 prior years
• $5,000 expected current year

Contributions 2026:
• Employee: $17,000 ($3,500 catch-up)
• Employer: 2% nonelective OR 3% match

No other qualified plans allowed
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: SEP-IRA Contribution Limits
flashcards.back:
Simplified Employee Pension:

• Employer-only contributions
• Up to 25% of compensation
• Maximum $71,500 (2026)
• Self-employed: net SE × 0.9235 × 20%

Eligibility: 21+, 3 of last 5 years, $750 comp

Simplest employer plan to establish
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: Roth IRA 5-Year Rules
flashcards.back:
TWO 5-year rules:

1. CONVERSIONS: 5-year wait per conversion
   (avoids 10% penalty under 59½)

2. EARNINGS: Account open 5 years
   (for tax-free qualified distribution)

Qualified = 5+ years AND:
• Age 59½, OR
• Death, disability, OR
• First home ($10K lifetime)
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: What are the FOUR risk management techniques?
flashcards.back:
Risk Management Techniques:

1. AVOID: Eliminate the risk entirely
2. REDUCE: Minimize likelihood/severity
3. RETAIN: Accept the risk (self-insure)
4. TRANSFER: Shift to another party (insurance)

"ARRT" - Avoid, Reduce, Retain, Transfer

Choice depends on frequency and severity

flashcards.front: What is PURE vs SPECULATIVE risk?
flashcards.back:
PURE RISK:
• Only possible outcomes: Loss or no loss
• Insurable
• Examples: Fire, accident, death

SPECULATIVE RISK:
• Possible outcomes: Loss, no change, or gain
• Generally NOT insurable
• Examples: Investments, starting a business

Insurance typically covers pure risks only
flashcards.example: Financial Independence: Save 50% of income, invest aggressively, target 25× annual expenses ($1M for $40K/year spending).

flashcards.front: What are the SIX elements of an insurable risk?
flashcards.back:
Insurable Risk Requirements:
1. LARGE NUMBER: Law of large numbers
2. ACCIDENTAL: Not intentional
3. DEFINITE LOSS: Measurable, time, amount
4. NOT CATASTROPHIC: Spread risk geographically
5. ECONOMICALLY FEASIBLE: Affordable premium
6. CALCULABLE: Can estimate frequency/severity

Insurance companies need these to price policies

flashcards.front: What is INSURABLE INTEREST?
flashcards.back:
Insurable Interest:
• Must suffer financial loss if insured event occurs
• Prevents gambling/wagering
• Prevents moral hazard

Life Insurance:
• Required at inception only
• Presumed: Self, spouse, dependents, business partners

Property Insurance:
• Required at inception AND at time of loss
• Only paid to extent of interest
flashcards.example: Term: $1M coverage, 20-year term, $50/month. Whole life: $500,000 coverage, $400/month, builds cash value.

flashcards.front: What is the PRINCIPLE OF INDEMNITY?
flashcards.back:
Principle of Indemnity:
• Restore insured to pre-loss position
• Cannot profit from a loss
• Prevents moral hazard

Applies to:
• Property insurance
• Liability insurance
• Health insurance (mostly)

Exceptions:
• Life insurance (valued policy)
• Agreed value policies
flashcards.example: Term: $1M coverage, 20-year term, $50/month. Whole life: $500,000 coverage, $400/month, builds cash value.

flashcards.front: What is SUBROGATION?
flashcards.back: Subrogation:\n• Insurer steps into insured

flashcards.front: What is TERM LIFE insurance?
flashcards.back:
Term Life Insurance:
• Pure death protection
• Coverage for specific period
• No cash value
• Lowest initial premium

Types:
• LEVEL TERM: Same premium throughout
• ANNUAL RENEWABLE (ART): Increases yearly
• DECREASING TERM: Declining benefit

Options: Renewable, convertible
Best for: Temporary, maximum coverage needs
flashcards.example: Term: $1M coverage, 20-year term, $50/month. Whole life: $500,000 coverage, $400/month, builds cash value.

flashcards.front: What is WHOLE LIFE insurance?
flashcards.back:
Whole Life Insurance:
• Permanent coverage (to age 100+)
• Level premium
• Cash value growth (guaranteed)
• Fixed death benefit

Cash Value:
• Tax-deferred growth
• Access via loans or withdrawals
• Reduces death benefit if outstanding

Higher premiums than term
Guaranteed by insurer
flashcards.example: Term: $1M coverage, 20-year term, $50/month. Whole life: $500,000 coverage, $400/month, builds cash value.

flashcards.front: What is UNIVERSAL LIFE insurance?
flashcards.back:
Universal Life Insurance:
• Flexible premiums within limits
• Adjustable death benefit
• Cash value with credited interest
• Transparency (unbundled costs)

Types:
• TRADITIONAL UL: Interest rate based
• INDEXED UL (IUL): Tied to market index
• VARIABLE UL (VUL): Investment subaccounts

GUARANTEED UL: No cash value, guaranteed death benefit
flashcards.example: Term: $1M coverage, 20-year term, $50/month. Whole life: $500,000 coverage, $400/month, builds cash value.

flashcards.front: How is LIFE INSURANCE taxation handled?
flashcards.back:
Life Insurance Taxation:

DEATH BENEFIT:
• Generally income tax-free
• May be in estate for estate tax

CASH VALUE:
• Growth is tax-deferred
• Loans not taxable (if policy stays in force)
• Withdrawals: FIFO (basis first)
• Surrender: Gain taxed as ordinary income

MEC: LIFO treatment (earnings first)
flashcards.example: 2024 exemption: $13.61M per person. Estate of $15M: taxable amount $1.39M × 40% = $556,000 federal estate tax.

flashcards.front: What is a MODIFIED ENDOWMENT CONTRACT (MEC)?
flashcards.back:
Modified Endowment Contract:
• Fails 7-pay test
• Overfunded life insurance

Tax Consequences:
• LIFO: Earnings withdrawn first
• Taxable as ordinary income
• 10% penalty before 59½
• Still tax-free death benefit

7-Pay Test: Premium > 7-year premium for paid-up
Cannot "unmake" a MEC
flashcards.example: Term: $1M coverage, 20-year term, $50/month. Whole life: $500,000 coverage, $400/month, builds cash value.

flashcards.front: What is an IRREVOCABLE LIFE INSURANCE TRUST (ILIT)?
flashcards.back: ILIT:\n• Trust owns life insurance policy\n• Removes from insured
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: What are LIFE INSURANCE settlement options?
flashcards.back:
Settlement Options:

1. LUMP SUM: One-time payment
2. INTEREST ONLY: Pay interest, hold principal
3. FIXED PERIOD: Equal payments over time
4. FIXED AMOUNT: Specific amount until depleted
5. LIFE ANNUITY: Lifetime payments
6. LIFE WITH PERIOD CERTAIN: Life or minimum term

Beneficiary chooses (or owner designates)

flashcards.front: How much LIFE INSURANCE is needed?
flashcards.back:
Needs Analysis Methods:

HUMAN LIFE VALUE:
• Present value of future earnings
• Less taxes and personal consumption

NEEDS APPROACH:
• Immediate needs (final expenses, debts)
• Income replacement needs
• Less existing resources

Rule of thumb: 10-15x income
Consider: Debt, children, spouse income, goals
flashcards.example: To have $50,000 in 10 years at 5% return: PV = $50,000 / (1.05)^10 = $30,696 needed today.

flashcards.front: What is OWN OCCUPATION vs ANY OCCUPATION disability?
flashcards.back:
Disability Definitions:

OWN OCCUPATION:
• Cannot perform your specific job
• Most favorable to insured
• Can work elsewhere, still collect
• Higher premiums

ANY OCCUPATION:
• Cannot perform any job suited to education/experience
• More restrictive
• Lower premiums

Some policies: Own-occ for period, then any-occ

flashcards.front: What is the ELIMINATION PERIOD?
flashcards.back:
Elimination Period:
• Waiting period before benefits begin
• Like a deductible (time, not $)
• Common: 30, 60, 90, 180 days

Longer elimination period = Lower premium

Considerations:
• Emergency fund/savings
• Sick leave / PTO
• Short-term disability coverage
• Cash flow needs
flashcards.example: 3-6 months expenses. Client spends $5,000/month: maintain $15,000-$30,000 in liquid savings.

flashcards.front: What DISABILITY INSURANCE riders are important?
flashcards.back:
Important DI Riders:

• COLA: Increases benefits with inflation
• FUTURE PURCHASE: Buy more without medical
• RESIDUAL/PARTIAL: Proportional benefits
• OWN-OCCUPATION: Specific job definition
• NON-CANCELABLE: Guaranteed premiums
• GUARANTEED RENEWABLE: Must renew
• RETURN OF PREMIUM: Get premiums back

Riders add cost but value
flashcards.example: Client earns $120,000. Policy: 60% income replacement, 90-day elimination period, benefits to age 65.

flashcards.front: How is DISABILITY INCOME taxed?
flashcards.back:
Disability Income Taxation:

INDIVIDUAL POLICY (you pay premiums):
• Benefits are TAX-FREE

EMPLOYER-PAID POLICY:
• Benefits are TAXABLE as income

MIXED FUNDING:
• Employee portion: Tax-free
• Employer portion: Taxable

Tip: Consider paying own premiums for tax-free benefits

flashcards.front: What does LONG-TERM CARE insurance cover?
flashcards.back: Long-Term Care Coverage:\n• Assistance with activities of daily living (ADLs)\n• Cognitive impairment (dementia, Alzheimer
flashcards.example: Policy: $200/day benefit, 3-year benefit period, 90-day elimination. Lifetime benefit: $219,000.

flashcards.front: What LTC POLICY features are important?
flashcards.back:
Key LTC Policy Features:

• DAILY BENEFIT: $100-$500/day typical
• BENEFIT PERIOD: 2-5 years or lifetime
• ELIMINATION PERIOD: 0-90 days
• INFLATION PROTECTION: 3-5% compound
• TAX-QUALIFIED: Deductible premiums

Partnership policies: Asset protection option
Hybrid policies: LTC + life/annuity combo

flashcards.front: How is LTC INSURANCE taxed?
flashcards.back:
LTC Insurance Taxation:

TAX-QUALIFIED POLICY:
• Premiums: Deductible (age-based limits)
• Benefits: Generally tax-free
• Must meet ADL/cognitive triggers

NON-QUALIFIED:
• Premiums: Not deductible
• Benefits: May be tax-free

2024 Deductible Limits (example):
• Age 40-50: $1,790
• Age 60-70: $4,770
• Over 70: $5,960

flashcards.front: What is a PPO vs HMO?
flashcards.back:
PPO (Preferred Provider Organization):
• Network of providers
• No referrals needed
• Out-of-network allowed (higher cost)
• Higher premiums

HMO (Health Maintenance Organization):
• Requires primary care physician (PCP)
• Referrals required for specialists
• No out-of-network coverage
• Lower premiums

flashcards.front: What is a HIGH DEDUCTIBLE HEALTH PLAN (HDHP)?
flashcards.back:
HDHP Requirements (2024):
• INDIVIDUAL: $1,600+ deductible / $8,050 max OOP
• FAMILY: $3,200+ deductible / $16,100 max OOP

Features:
• Lower premiums
• Higher cost-sharing
• Required for HSA eligibility
• Preventive care covered pre-deductible

Best for: Healthy individuals, HSA savers

flashcards.front: What is the difference between HSA, FSA, and HRA?
flashcards.back:
Health Spending Accounts:

HSA:
• Requires HDHP
• Employee-owned, portable
• Rolls over indefinitely
• Triple tax benefit

FSA:
• Any health plan
• Employer-owned
• Use-it-or-lose-it (mostly)

HRA:
• Employer-funded only
• Employer-owned
• May roll over
• Various designs

flashcards.front: What are COBRA requirements?
flashcards.back:
COBRA:
• Consolidated Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act
• Continue employer coverage after qualifying event

Requirements:
• 20+ employees
• 102% of premium (employer + employee)

Duration:
• Termination/reduced hours: 18 months
• Death, divorce, Medicare: 36 months
• Disability: 29 months

No new coverage requirement

flashcards.front: What are ACA ESSENTIAL HEALTH BENEFITS?
flashcards.back:
ACA Essential Health Benefits (10):
1. Ambulatory patient services
2. Emergency services
3. Hospitalization
4. Pregnancy, maternity, newborn
5. Mental health, substance use
6. Prescription drugs
7. Rehabilitative services
8. Laboratory services
9. Preventive/wellness
10. Pediatric services (dental, vision)

No annual/lifetime dollar limits

flashcards.front: What is HOMEOWNERS INSURANCE (HO-3)?
flashcards.back:
HO-3 (Special Form):
• Most common homeowners policy

Dwelling: Open perils (all risks)
Personal Property: Named perils only

Coverage Parts:
• A: Dwelling
• B: Other structures (10% of A)
• C: Personal property (50% of A)
• D: Loss of use
• E: Personal liability
• F: Medical payments

flashcards.front: What is REPLACEMENT COST vs ACTUAL CASH VALUE?
flashcards.back:
Property Valuation:

REPLACEMENT COST (RC):
• Cost to replace with new item
• No depreciation deducted
• Higher premiums

ACTUAL CASH VALUE (ACV):
• Replacement cost minus depreciation
• ACV = RC - Depreciation
• Lower payouts, lower premiums

Recommendation: Choose RC for dwelling and contents

flashcards.front: What is the 80% COINSURANCE rule?
flashcards.back:
Coinsurance Clause:
• Requires coverage of 80%+ of replacement cost
• Penalty for underinsurance

Formula: Claim Payment = Loss × (Amount Carried / Amount Required)

Example: $300K home, $200K coverage, $50K loss
• Required: $240K (80%)
• Payment: $50K × (200K/240K) = $41,667

Carry 80% or more to avoid penalty

flashcards.front: What does UMBRELLA insurance cover?
flashcards.back:
Umbrella (Excess Liability) Insurance:
• Extra liability above underlying limits
• Covers: Home, auto, watercraft, etc.
• Typically $1M-$10M limits
• Relatively inexpensive

Covers:
• Lawsuits, judgments
• Defense costs
• Drop-down coverage (some claims)

Requires underlying policies meet minimums
Best for: High net worth, higher risk exposure

flashcards.front: What are the parts of AUTO INSURANCE?
flashcards.back:
Auto Insurance Coverages:

• LIABILITY: Bodily injury (BI) & property damage (PD)
• MEDICAL PAYMENTS: Your medical bills
• UNINSURED/UNDERINSURED (UM/UIM): Other driver lacks coverage
• COLLISION: Damage from impact
• COMPREHENSIVE (Other Than Collision): Theft, weather, animals

Limits shown as: 100/300/50
(BI per person/BI per accident/PD)

flashcards.front: What is NO-FAULT auto insurance?
flashcards.back:
No-Fault Auto Insurance:
• Your insurer pays your injuries
• Regardless of who caused accident
• Personal Injury Protection (PIP)
• Limits right to sue (threshold)

States: About 12 require no-fault

Advantages:
• Faster claims
• Lower legal costs

Disadvantages:
• May limit recovery
• Potentially higher premiums

flashcards.front: What is PROFESSIONAL LIABILITY insurance?
flashcards.back:
Professional Liability (E&O):
• Errors and Omissions coverage
• Protects against negligence claims
• Covers advice/service failures

Types:
• Medical Malpractice: Healthcare providers
• Legal Malpractice: Attorneys
• E&O: Financial advisors, accountants
• D&O: Directors and officers

Claims-made vs occurrence basis

flashcards.front: What is CLAIMS-MADE vs OCCURRENCE coverage?
flashcards.back:
Liability Policy Types:

CLAIMS-MADE:
• Covers claims made during policy period
• Must have coverage when claim filed
• Need tail coverage if policy ends
• Usually professional liability

OCCURRENCE:
• Covers events during policy period
• Claim can be made later
• No tail needed
• Usually general liability

Occurrence more secure for insured

flashcards.front: What is BUSINESS OWNERS POLICY (BOP)?
flashcards.back:
Business Owners Policy:
• Package policy for small businesses
• Combines property + liability
• Similar to homeowners for businesses

Covers:
• Building and contents
• Business income
• General liability
• Crime, equipment breakdown (options)

Eligibility: Small to mid-size businesses
Lower cost than separate policies

flashcards.front: What is KEY PERSON life insurance?
flashcards.back:
Key Person Insurance:
• Business owns policy on key employee
• Business is beneficiary
• Compensates for loss of key person

Uses:
• Replace lost profit
• Recruit/train replacement
• Pay off debts
• Reassure creditors

Premiums: Not deductible
Death benefit: Tax-free to business
flashcards.example: Term: $1M coverage, 20-year term, $50/month. Whole life: $500,000 coverage, $400/month, builds cash value.

flashcards.front: What is a BUY-SELL agreement?
flashcards.back:
Buy-Sell Agreement:
• Provides for business ownership transfer
• At death, disability, or other trigger

Funded by:
• Life insurance
• Disability buyout insurance

Types:
• CROSS-PURCHASE: Owners buy each other out
• ENTITY (STOCK REDEMPTION): Business buys
• WAIT AND SEE: Decide at trigger

Sets valuation formula or method
flashcards.example: Term: $1M coverage, 20-year term, $50/month. Whole life: $500,000 coverage, $400/month, builds cash value.

flashcards.front: What are the TYPES of annuities?
flashcards.back:
Annuity Types:

BY PREMIUM:
• Single premium
• Flexible premium

BY PAYOUT START:
• Immediate: Payments begin within year
• Deferred: Accumulation period first

BY RETURN:
• Fixed: Guaranteed rate
• Variable: Investment-based
• Indexed: Market index linked

BY PAYOUT:
• Period certain, life, joint

flashcards.front: How are ANNUITY distributions taxed?
flashcards.back:
Annuity Taxation:

ACCUMULATION:
• Tax-deferred growth
• No annual limit (nonqualified)

WITHDRAWALS (Nonqualified):
• LIFO: Earnings first = taxable
• Principal return = tax-free
• 10% penalty before 59½

ANNUITIZATION:
• Exclusion ratio applies
• Part return of principal

1035 exchange for tax-free transfer

flashcards.front: What is the EXCLUSION RATIO for annuities?
flashcards.back: Exclusion Ratio:\n• Portion of annuity payment that

flashcards.front: What is MEDIGAP (Medicare Supplement)?
flashcards.back:
Medigap (Medicare Supplement):
• Private insurance to cover Medicare gaps
• Standardized plans (A, B, C, D, F, G, K, L, M, N)
• Covers deductibles, copays, coinsurance

Rules:
• Must have Part A and B
• Cannot use with Medicare Advantage
• Guaranteed issue during open enrollment
• No pre-existing condition exclusions (OEP)

Plan G most popular (F closed to new enrollees)

flashcards.front: What is IRMAA for Medicare?
flashcards.back:
IRMAA (Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount):
• Higher Part B and D premiums for high earners
• Based on MAGI from 2 years prior

2024 Thresholds (single):
• $103,000+: Surcharge begins
• $500,000+: Maximum surcharge

Planning:
• Roth conversions can increase IRMAA
• IRMAA appeals for life-changing events
• Manage income 2 years before Medicare
flashcards.example: Convert $50,000 traditional IRA to Roth, pay $11,000 tax (22% bracket). Future growth tax-free.

flashcards.front: What are SOCIAL SECURITY disability benefits?
flashcards.back:
SSDI (Social Security Disability Insurance):
• Based on work credits (usually 40)
• Strict disability definition
• 5-month waiting period
• Amount based on earnings history

SSI (Supplemental Security Income):
• Need-based, not work-based
• Disabled, blind, or 65+
• Limited income and resources
• Flat benefit rate
flashcards.example: Full retirement age 67, monthly benefit $2,500. Claiming at 62: $2,500 × 0.7 = $1,750/month (30% reduction).

flashcards.front: What is a PERSONAL RISK MANAGEMENT process?
flashcards.back:
Personal Risk Management Steps:

1. IDENTIFY: List potential risks
   • Life, health, property, liability

2. EVALUATE: Assess frequency and severity

3. SELECT TECHNIQUE:
   • Avoid, reduce, retain, transfer

4. IMPLEMENT: Purchase coverage, take actions

5. MONITOR: Review periodically, adjust

Prioritize high severity risks

flashcards.front: What is a GRACE PERIOD?
flashcards.back:
Grace Period:
• Time to pay premium after due date
• Coverage continues during grace period
• Prevents immediate lapse

Typical Periods:
• Life insurance: 30-31 days
• Health insurance: 10-31 days
• Auto/Property: Varies by state

Payment during grace period:
• Reinstates without underwriting
• May charge interest
flashcards.example: Term: $1M coverage, 20-year term, $50/month. Whole life: $500,000 coverage, $400/month, builds cash value.

flashcards.front: What is the INCONTESTABILITY clause?
flashcards.back: Incontestability Clause:\n• Limits insurer

flashcards.front: What are common LIFE INSURANCE policy riders?
flashcards.back:
Life Insurance Riders:

• WAIVER OF PREMIUM: Waive if disabled
• ACCELERATED DEATH BENEFIT: Terminal illness
• ACCIDENTAL DEATH: Extra benefit (AD&D)
• GUARANTEED INSURABILITY: Future purchase
• CHILD TERM: Cover children
• LONG-TERM CARE: Access for LTC needs
• RETURN OF PREMIUM: Get premiums back

Riders add cost but flexibility
flashcards.example: Term: $1M coverage, 20-year term, $50/month. Whole life: $500,000 coverage, $400/month, builds cash value.

flashcards.front: What does WORKERS COMPENSATION cover?
flashcards.back:
Workers Compensation:
• State-mandated employer coverage
• Work-related injuries and illnesses

Benefits:
• Medical expenses
• Disability income (partial wage replacement)
• Death benefits
• Rehabilitation

No-fault system:
• Employee gives up right to sue
• Receives benefits regardless of fault

Employer pays 100% of premium

flashcards.front: What does TITLE INSURANCE protect against?
flashcards.back:
Title Insurance:
• Protects against defects in property title
• One-time premium at closing

Covers:
• Forgery
• Undisclosed heirs
• Recording errors
• Liens and encumbrances
• Boundary disputes

Types:
• OWNER

flashcards.front: What is FLOOD INSURANCE?
flashcards.back:
Flood Insurance:
• NOT covered by homeowners (HO-3)
• NFIP: National Flood Insurance Program
• Private flood policies also available

NFIP Limits:
• Dwelling: $250,000
• Contents: $100,000

Requirements:
• Community must participate
• Required in flood zones with federally-backed mortgages
• 30-day waiting period

flashcards.front: What is EARTHQUAKE INSURANCE?
flashcards.back:
Earthquake Insurance:
• NOT covered by standard homeowners
• Separate policy or endorsement
• High deductibles (10-20% of coverage)

Covers:
• Dwelling damage
• Personal property
• Additional living expenses

Most important in:
• California
• Pacific Northwest
• New Madrid fault zone

Premium based on construction, location

flashcards.front: What does RENTERS INSURANCE cover?
flashcards.back:
Renters Insurance (HO-4):
• Personal property protection
• Liability coverage
• Additional living expenses

Does NOT cover:
• Building structure (landlord

flashcards.front: What is VIATICAL vs LIFE SETTLEMENT?
flashcards.back:
Life Policy Settlements:

VIATICAL SETTLEMENT:
• Terminally ill (2 years or less)
• Sell policy for % of death benefit
• Proceeds generally tax-free
• Original purpose of accelerated benefits

LIFE SETTLEMENT:
• Not terminally ill (usually 65+)
• Sell unwanted policy
• Proceeds may be taxable
• Buyer becomes beneficiary

flashcards.front: What is BUSINESS INCOME insurance?
flashcards.back:
Business Income (Business Interruption):
• Replaces lost income during covered loss
• Covers: Net income + continuing expenses

Covers:
• Loss of revenue
• Payroll continuation
• Loan payments
• Rent
• Relocation costs

Waiting period: Usually 72 hours
Coinsurance typically applies

flashcards.front: What is NEGLIGENCE?
flashcards.back:
Negligence Elements (4):

1. DUTY: Owed to injured party
2. BREACH: Failed to meet standard of care
3. PROXIMATE CAUSE: Breach caused injury
4. DAMAGES: Actual harm occurred

Must prove all four elements

Defenses:
• Comparative/contributory negligence
• Assumption of risk
• Statute of limitations

flashcards.front: What is COMPARATIVE vs CONTRIBUTORY negligence?
flashcards.back:
Negligence Doctrines:

CONTRIBUTORY:
• Any fault by plaintiff bars recovery
• Very harsh rule
• Few states use

COMPARATIVE:
• Damages reduced by plaintiff

flashcards.front: What factors for selecting INSURANCE COMPANY?
flashcards.back:
Insurance Company Selection:

FINANCIAL STRENGTH:
• AM Best, S&P, Moody

flashcards.front: What is GUARANTEED RENEWABILITY?
flashcards.back:
Renewability Options:

GUARANTEED RENEWABLE:
• Must renew policy
• Cannot cancel (except non-payment)
• CAN raise premiums (class basis)

NON-CANCELABLE:
• Must renew
• CANNOT raise premiums
• Best for disability

CONDITIONALLY RENEWABLE:
• Can decline renewal for specific reasons

Optionally Renewable: Insurer chooses

flashcards.front: What is a BENEFICIARY DESIGNATION best practice?
flashcards.back:
Beneficiary Best Practices:

• Name PRIMARY and CONTINGENT
• Be specific (name, DOB, SSN)
• Avoid "estate" as beneficiary
• Review regularly (divorce, death)
• Consider per stirpes vs per capita
• Coordinate with estate plan
• Minor beneficiaries need guardian/trust
• Irrevocable requires consent to change
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: Life Insurance Needs Analysis
flashcards.back:
D - Debt (mortgages, loans, credit cards)
I - Income replacement (future earnings)
M - Mortgage (separate if large)
E - Education (children
flashcards.example: Term: $1M coverage, 20-year term, $50/month. Whole life: $500,000 coverage, $400/month, builds cash value.

flashcards.front: Disability Insurance - Definition of Disability
flashcards.back:
Three definitions (strongest to weakest):

1. OWN OCCUPATION (best)
   - Can"t perform YOUR specific job
   - Highest premiums

2. MODIFIED OWN-OCC
   - Own-occ for 2-5 years, then any-occ

3. ANY OCCUPATION (worst)
   - Can"t perform ANY job suited to education/experience
   - Lowest premiums, hardest to collect
flashcards.example: Client earns $120,000. Policy: 60% income replacement, 90-day elimination period, benefits to age 65.

flashcards.front: Homeowners Insurance Coverage Forms
flashcards.back:
HO-3 (Most Common):
• Dwelling: OPEN perils (all risks)
• Contents: NAMED perils only

HO-5 (Comprehensive):
• Both dwelling AND contents: OPEN perils
• More expensive

HO-4: Renters (contents only)
HO-6: Condo (interior only)

flashcards.front: COBRA Continuation Coverage Periods
flashcards.back:
18 months: General qualifying event
• Termination (not gross misconduct)
• Reduction in hours

36 months: Extended events
• Death of employee
• Divorce/separation
• Medicare entitlement
• Dependent child aging out

102% of premium (employer pays nothing)

flashcards.front: What are the FIVE tax filing statuses?
flashcards.back:
Filing Statuses:
1. SINGLE - Unmarried
2. MARRIED FILING JOINTLY - Combined return
3. MARRIED FILING SEPARATELY - Individual returns
4. HEAD OF HOUSEHOLD - Unmarried with qualifying person
5. QUALIFYING SURVIVING SPOUSE - 2 years after spouse death with dependent

flashcards.front: What are HEAD OF HOUSEHOLD requirements?
flashcards.back:
Head of Household Requirements:
• Unmarried (or considered unmarried)
• Pay >50% household costs
• Qualifying person lived with you >½ year
• NOT a qualifying surviving spouse

Qualifying persons:
• Qualifying child
• Qualifying relative (certain)
• Dependent parent (can live elsewhere)

flashcards.front: What is GROSS INCOME?
flashcards.back:
Gross Income: All income from whatever source derived

Includes:
• Wages, salaries, tips
• Interest and dividends
• Business income
• Capital gains
• Rents, royalties
• Alimony (pre-2019)
• Retirement distributions
• Gambling winnings

Excludes: Gifts, inheritance, municipal bond interest
