flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What is ADJUSTED GROSS INCOME (AGI)?
flashcards.back:
AGI = Gross Income - Above-the-Line Deductions

Above-the-Line (Part II of Schedule 1):
• IRA contributions
• Student loan interest
• HSA contributions
• Self-employment tax (½)
• Self-employed health insurance
• Alimony paid (pre-2019)
• Moving expenses (military)
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: What is the STANDARD DEDUCTION for 2026?
flashcards.back:
2026 Standard Deduction:
• Single: $15,350
• MFJ: $30,700
• MFS: $15,350
• HOH: $22,900

Additional for 65+/blind:
• Single/HOH: +$2,050 each
• MFJ/MFS: +$1,650 each

Note: Cannot take if itemizing

flashcards.front: What are the main ITEMIZED DEDUCTIONS?
flashcards.back:
Schedule A Itemized Deductions:
• Medical/dental (>7.5% AGI)
• State/local taxes (SALT $10K cap)
• Mortgage interest ($750K limit)
• Charitable contributions (60% AGI cash)
• Casualty/theft losses (federally declared)

Note: Misc deductions eliminated (TCJA)

flashcards.front: What is the SALT deduction limit?
flashcards.back:
SALT (State and Local Tax) Deduction:
• Maximum: $10,000 ($5,000 MFS)
• Includes:
  - State income OR sales tax
  - Property taxes
• TCJA change (2018-2025)
• May revert after 2025
• Big impact on high-tax states

flashcards.front: What are CHARITABLE CONTRIBUTION limits?
flashcards.back:
Charitable Contribution Limits (% of AGI):
• Cash to public charity: 60%
• Appreciated property: 30%
• Cash to private foundation: 30%
• Appreciated to private: 20%

Carryforward: 5 years
Must itemize to deduct
Need documentation over $250

flashcards.front: What is the CHILD TAX CREDIT for 2024?
flashcards.back:
Child Tax Credit 2024:
• $2,000 per qualifying child under 17
• Up to $1,600 refundable (ACTC)
• Phaseout: $400K MFJ, $200K others
• $500 credit for other dependents

Requirements:
• Under 17 at year-end
• SSN required
• Lived with taxpayer >½ year

flashcards.front: What is the EARNED INCOME TAX CREDIT (EITC)?
flashcards.back:
EITC Features:
• Refundable credit for low-income workers
• Based on earned income and children
• 2024 max: ~$7,830 (3+ children)
• Phase-in, plateau, phase-out structure
• Must have earned income
• Investment income limit: $11,600
• Filing threshold required

flashcards.front: What is the CHILD AND DEPENDENT CARE CREDIT?
flashcards.back:
Dependent Care Credit:
• For work-related child/dependent care
• Expenses limit: $3,000 (1), $6,000 (2+)
• Credit: 20-35% of expenses
• Children under 13
• Non-refundable
• Reduced by FSA benefits
• Both spouses must work (or student)

flashcards.front: What are LONG-TERM capital gain tax rates?
flashcards.back:
Long-Term Capital Gains (held >1 year):
• 0%: Up to $47,025 single / $94,050 MFJ
• 15%: $47,025-$518,900 single / $94,050-$583,750 MFJ
• 20%: Above those thresholds

• 25%: Unrecaptured Sec 1250 (depreciation)
• 28%: Collectibles and QSBS

flashcards.front: What is the NET INVESTMENT INCOME TAX (NIIT)?
flashcards.back:
NIIT (3.8% Surtax):
• Applies to investment income
• When MAGI exceeds:
  - $250,000 MFJ
  - $200,000 Single/HOH
  - $125,000 MFS

Investment income includes:
• Interest, dividends
• Capital gains
• Rental/royalty income
• Passive business income

flashcards.front: What is WASH SALE rule?
flashcards.back:
Wash Sale Rule:
• Cannot deduct loss if:
  - Buy substantially identical security
  - Within 30 days before or after sale
• Disallowed loss added to new basis
• Holding period carries over
• Applies to stocks, bonds, options
• Does NOT apply to gains

flashcards.front: What is the capital loss LIMITATION?
flashcards.back:
Capital Loss Rules:
• Offset capital gains first
• Then up to $3,000 ordinary income ($1,500 MFS)
• Excess carries forward indefinitely
• Short-term losses offset ST gains first
• Long-term losses offset LT gains first
• Then net against each other

flashcards.front: What is the ALTERNATIVE MINIMUM TAX (AMT)?
flashcards.back:
AMT:
• Parallel tax system
• Pay higher of regular tax or AMT
• Add back certain deductions (SALT, misc)
• Exempt amount phases out

2024 Exemptions:
• Single: $85,700 (phaseout at $609,350)
• MFJ: $133,300 (phaseout at $1,218,700)

Rates: 26% / 28%

flashcards.front: What items trigger AMT?
flashcards.back:
AMT Triggers (Add-backs):
• State/local taxes (SALT)
• Miscellaneous deductions (if any)
• Interest on private activity bonds
• Incentive stock option exercise (spread)
• Accelerated depreciation
• Certain passive losses
• Standard deduction (not allowed)
• Personal exemptions (not allowed)

flashcards.front: How is SELF-EMPLOYMENT TAX calculated?
flashcards.back:
Self-Employment Tax:
• 15.3% on first $168,600 (2024)
  - 12.4% Social Security
  - 2.9% Medicare
• 2.9% Medicare on all earnings above
• 0.9% Additional Medicare over $200K/$250K

Calculation:
• Net SE income × 92.35% × rates
• Deduct ½ SE tax above the line
flashcards.example: Full retirement age 67, monthly benefit $2,500. Claiming at 62: $2,500 × 0.7 = $1,750/month (30% reduction).

flashcards.front: What is the QBI DEDUCTION (Section 199A)?
flashcards.back:
Qualified Business Income Deduction:
• Up to 20% of qualified business income
• Pass-through entities, sole props, S-corps
• Deduction = lesser of:
  - 20% of QBI, OR
  - 20% of taxable income (before QBI)

Limitations for high earners:
• W-2 wage limit
• Specified service business (SSTB) limit

flashcards.front: What is a SPECIFIED SERVICE TRADE OR BUSINESS (SSTB)?
flashcards.back:
SSTB (Limited QBI deduction):
• Health, law, accounting
• Actuarial, performing arts
• Consulting, athletics, financial services
• Brokerage services
• Any business relying on reputation/skill

Excluded from SSTB:
• Engineering, architecture

Phaseout: $191,950 single / $383,900 MFJ

flashcards.front: What is SECTION 179 expensing?
flashcards.back:
Section 179 Deduction:
• Immediate expense certain business property
• 2024 limit: $1,220,000
• Phaseout begins: $3,050,000
• Applies to: Equipment, machinery, software, vehicles
• Limited to business income
• Excess carries forward
• SUV limit: $30,500

flashcards.front: What is BONUS DEPRECIATION?
flashcards.back:
Bonus Depreciation:
• Additional first-year depreciation
• 2024: 60% (was 100% in 2022)
• Phases down 20%/year through 2027
• New and used property eligible
• No income limit (unlike 179)
• Can create/increase NOL
• Certain property excluded

flashcards.front: What are PASSIVE ACTIVITY loss rules?
flashcards.back:
Passive Activity Rules (§469):
• Passive losses only offset passive income
• Cannot offset active or portfolio income
• Suspended losses carry forward
• Released at disposition

Passive Activities:
• Rentals (generally)
• Limited partnerships
• Business without material participation

flashcards.front: What is the $25,000 RENTAL REAL ESTATE exception?
flashcards.back:
Active Participation Exception:
• Up to $25,000 rental loss deduction
• Must actively participate
• Own at least 10%
• Phaseout: $100,000-$150,000 MAGI
• Reduced $1 for every $2 over $100K
• Eliminated at $150K MAGI

Real estate professional exception: No passive limits

flashcards.front: What are ESTIMATED TAX payment rules?
flashcards.back:
Estimated Tax Requirements:
• Pay if owing $1,000+ at filing
• Due: April 15, June 15, Sept 15, Jan 15

Safe Harbors (no penalty):
• 90% of current year, OR
• 100% of prior year (110% if AGI >$150K)

Penalty calculated per quarter
No penalty if <$1,000 due

flashcards.front: What is TAX BASIS and how is it calculated?
flashcards.back:
Tax Basis = Cost + Improvements - Depreciation

Basis Sources:
• Purchase: Cost + commissions
• Gift: Generally donor

flashcards.front: How does STEP-UP IN BASIS work?
flashcards.back:
Step-Up in Basis:
• Inherited property: New basis = FMV at death
• Prior capital gains erased
• Can also step down if worth less

Community Property:
• Full step-up on both halves

Joint Tenancy (non-CP):
• Only decedent

flashcards.front: What is GIFT TAX BASIS?
flashcards.back: Gift Basis (Carryover Basis):\n\nIf FMV ≥ donor
flashcards.example: Annual exclusion $18,000 (2024). Parent gifts $50,000: $32,000 uses lifetime exemption or triggers gift tax.

flashcards.front: Compare C-CORP vs S-CORP taxation
flashcards.back:
C-CORPORATION:
• Double taxation (corp level + dividends)
• 21% flat corporate rate
• Unlimited shareholders
• Retain earnings

S-CORPORATION:
• Pass-through (no entity tax)
• Income on K-1
• Max 100 shareholders
• One class of stock
• Can reduce SE tax

flashcards.front: What are the S-CORP requirements?
flashcards.back:
S-Corporation Requirements:
• Domestic corporation
• ≤100 shareholders
• Only individuals, estates, certain trusts
• One class of stock
• All shareholders consent
• Calendar year (generally)
• No nonresident alien shareholders
• File Form 2553 timely
flashcards.example: Revocable trust: client transfers home, avoids probate, retains control. Irrevocable: removes assets from estate but loses control.

flashcards.front: How are PARTNERSHIPS taxed?
flashcards.back:
Partnership Taxation:
• Pass-through entity
• No entity-level tax
• Income/loss on K-1
• Partners pay SE tax on guaranteed payments
• Outside basis (investment in partnership)
• Special allocations allowed
• Flexible distributions
• General and limited partners

flashcards.front: What is an LLC\
flashcards.back:
LLC Default Tax Treatment:

Single-Member:
• Disregarded (Schedule C)

Multi-Member:
• Partnership (Form 1065)

Can elect:
• S-corporation
• C-corporation

State law entity, federal tax flexibility

flashcards.front: What is the ANNUAL GIFT TAX EXCLUSION?
flashcards.back:
Annual Gift Tax Exclusion (2026):
• $19,000 per donee
• $38,000 if gift-splitting with spouse
• Unlimited recipients
• Present interest required
• Excludes educational payments (direct)
• Excludes medical payments (direct)
• No gift tax return if under limit
flashcards.example: Annual exclusion $18,000 (2024). Parent gifts $50,000: $32,000 uses lifetime exemption or triggers gift tax.

flashcards.front: What is the LIFETIME GIFT TAX EXEMPTION?
flashcards.back:
Unified Credit (2026):
• $7.0 million lifetime exemption (TCJA sunset)
• Applies to gifts and estate combined
• Portability between spouses
• Anti-clawback rule protects pre-2026 gifts

Gift tax rate: 40% on taxable gifts
flashcards.example: Annual exclusion $18,000 (2024). Parent gifts $50,000: $32,000 uses lifetime exemption or triggers gift tax.

flashcards.front: What is the KIDDIE TAX?
flashcards.back:
Kiddie Tax Rules (2024):
• Applies to children under 19 (under 24 if student)
• Unearned income over $2,600
  - First $1,300: Tax-free
  - Next $1,300: Child

flashcards.front: How are MUTUAL FUND distributions taxed?
flashcards.back:
Mutual Fund Taxation:

DIVIDENDS:
• Qualified: LTCG rates
• Non-qualified: Ordinary rates

CAPITAL GAINS:
• Short-term: Ordinary rates
• Long-term: LTCG rates

Note: Taxed regardless of reinvestment
Basis adjusted for reinvested distributions

flashcards.front: What are QUALIFIED DIVIDENDS?
flashcards.back:
Qualified Dividends:
• Taxed at LTCG rates (0%, 15%, 20%)

Requirements:
• Paid by US or qualified foreign corp
• Holding period: 60+ days around ex-date
• Not from preferred stock held <91 days

Not Qualified:
• REITs, money market, most foreign

flashcards.front: How is BOND interest taxed?
flashcards.back:
Bond Interest Taxation:

CORPORATE BONDS:
• Fully taxable as ordinary income

US TREASURY:
• Federal taxable
• State tax-exempt

MUNICIPAL BONDS:
• Federal tax-exempt
• State tax: Depends on where issued
• May trigger AMT (private activity)

flashcards.front: What is ORIGINAL ISSUE DISCOUNT (OID)?
flashcards.back:
Original Issue Discount:
• Bond issued below face value
• Difference = OID
• Amortize OID as interest income annually
• Phantom income (no cash received)
• Increases basis each year
• Report on Form 1099-OID

Example: Zero-coupon bonds

flashcards.front: How are ROTH IRA distributions taxed?
flashcards.back:
Roth IRA Distribution Taxation:

QUALIFIED (Tax-Free):
• 5-year holding period met
• Age 59½, death, disability, first home

NON-QUALIFIED:
• Contributions: Tax-free (already taxed)
• Earnings: Taxable + 10% penalty

Ordering: Contributions first, conversions, then earnings
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: How are TRADITIONAL IRA distributions taxed?
flashcards.back:
Traditional IRA Distribution Taxation:

FULLY DEDUCTIBLE CONTRIBUTIONS:
• 100% taxable as ordinary income
• 10% penalty if before 59½

NON-DEDUCTIBLE CONTRIBUTIONS:
• Pro-rata rule applies
• Portion is return of basis (tax-free)
• Remainder is taxable
• Track on Form 8606
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: What is a LIKE-KIND EXCHANGE (Section 1031)?
flashcards.back:
Section 1031 Exchange:
• Defer gain on real property exchange
• Must be held for business/investment
• Both properties must be like-kind
• 45 days to identify replacement
• 180 days to close
• Boot (cash/unlike) is taxable
• Basis carries over (adjusted for boot)

flashcards.front: What is DEPRECIATION RECAPTURE?
flashcards.back:
Depreciation Recapture:

SECTION 1245 (Personal Property):
• All depreciation recaptured as ordinary income

SECTION 1250 (Real Property):
• Straight-line: 25% rate (unrecaptured 1250)
• Excess over SL: Ordinary income

Recapture up to amount of gain

flashcards.front: What is a QUALIFIED OPPORTUNITY ZONE (QOZ)?
flashcards.back:
Opportunity Zone Benefits:

1. DEFERRAL: Defer capital gain invested in QOZ fund
2. REDUCTION: 10% basis step-up after 5 years
3. EXCLUSION: No tax on QOZ appreciation if held 10+ years

Must invest within 180 days of gain
Gain recognized by 2026 or earlier sale

flashcards.front: What is TAX LOSS HARVESTING?
flashcards.back:
Tax Loss Harvesting:
• Sell investments at loss
• Offset capital gains
• Up to $3,000 ordinary income
• Reinvest in similar (not identical) securities
• Avoid wash sale rule
• Year-end strategy
• Can increase long-term returns

flashcards.front: What is INCOME SHIFTING strategy?
flashcards.back:
Income Shifting Strategies:

FAMILY:
• Employ children (legitimate work)
• Gift income-producing property
• Family limited partnerships

TIMING:
• Defer income to lower bracket years
• Accelerate deductions
• Bunch charitable donations

Limitations: Kiddie tax, assignment of income

flashcards.front: What is BUNCHING deductions strategy?
flashcards.back:
Bunching Strategy:
• Concentrate deductions in alternate years
• Itemize when >standard deduction
• Standard deduction other years

Best for:
• Charitable contributions
• Medical expenses (if near 7.5%)
• State taxes (within $10K limit)

Use Donor Advised Fund for charity

flashcards.front: What are HSA contribution limits and tax benefits?
flashcards.back:
HSA (2026):

Contribution Limits:
• Self: $4,450
• Family: $8,900
• Catch-up (55+): +$1,000

Triple Tax Benefit:
• Contributions: Tax-deductible
• Growth: Tax-free
• Withdrawals: Tax-free (qualified medical)

Requires HDHP coverage

flashcards.front: What are common TAX PENALTIES?
flashcards.back:
Tax Penalties:
• Failure to file: 5%/month (max 25%)
• Failure to pay: 0.5%/month (max 25%)
• Accuracy: 20% of underpayment
• Fraud: 75% of underpayment
• Early withdrawal: 10% (retirement)
• Estimated tax: Variable by quarter

Reasonable cause can abate some penalties
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: How is ALIMONY taxed (Pre and Post 2019)?
flashcards.back:
Alimony Taxation:

PRE-2019 DIVORCES:
• Deductible by payer
• Taxable to recipient

POST-2018 DIVORCES:
• NOT deductible
• NOT taxable to recipient
• Same as child support

Note: Pre-2019 divorces can ELECT new rules

flashcards.front: What are QUALIFYING CHILD requirements?
flashcards.back:
Qualifying Child Tests:
• RELATIONSHIP: Child, step, foster, sibling
• AGE: Under 19 (24 if student), or disabled
• RESIDENCY: Same home >½ year
• SUPPORT: Not provide >½ own support
• JOINT RETURN: Cannot file joint (exception)

Tie-breaker rules if multiple claim

flashcards.front: What are QUALIFYING RELATIVE requirements?
flashcards.back:
Qualifying Relative Tests:
• NOT a qualifying child
• MEMBER of household OR relationship
• GROSS INCOME: <$5,050 (2024)
• SUPPORT: You provide >½
• NOT married filing jointly

Can be any age
Relatives: Parents, in-laws, aunts, uncles, etc.

flashcards.front: What is the SAVER\
flashcards.back:
Retirement Savings Contributions Credit:
• Credit for low-income retirement contributions
• 10%, 20%, or 50% of contribution
• Max contribution: $2,000 ($4,000 MFJ)
• Max credit: $1,000 ($2,000 MFJ)
• Non-refundable

2024 AGI limits:
• 50%: $23,000 single / $46,000 MFJ
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What are energy-related TAX CREDITS?
flashcards.back:
Energy Tax Credits (2024):

RESIDENTIAL CLEAN ENERGY:
• 30% of solar, wind, geothermal, battery
• No cap, through 2032

ENERGY EFFICIENT HOME:
• Up to $1,200/year
• Windows, doors, insulation, HVAC

ELECTRIC VEHICLE:
• Up to $7,500 new
• Up to $4,000 used

flashcards.front: What is a REAL ESTATE PROFESSIONAL for tax purposes?
flashcards.back:
Real Estate Professional Status:
• >750 hours in real property trades
• >50% of personal services
• Material participation in each activity

Benefits:
• Rental losses not passive
• Can offset ordinary income
• No $25K limitation
• Both spouses need not qualify

flashcards.front: How are INCENTIVE STOCK OPTIONS (ISOs) taxed?
flashcards.back:
ISO Taxation:

At EXERCISE:
• No regular tax (if hold)
• AMT: Spread is AMT income

At SALE (Qualifying Disposition):
• All gain is LTCG
• Must hold 2 years from grant, 1 year from exercise

Disqualifying Disposition:
• Spread at exercise = ordinary income
• Additional gain = capital

flashcards.front: How are NON-QUALIFIED STOCK OPTIONS (NQSOs) taxed?
flashcards.back:
NQSO Taxation:

At EXERCISE:
• Ordinary income = FMV - Strike price
• Subject to withholding
• Employer deduction

At SALE:
• Basis = FMV at exercise
• Gain/loss is capital
• ST or LT based on holding period

No AMT issues

flashcards.front: What employee benefits are TAX-FREE?
flashcards.back:
Tax-Free Employee Benefits:
• Employer health insurance premiums
• HSA contributions (employer)
• Life insurance (up to $50K)
• Dependent care (up to $5K FSA)
• Adoption assistance
• Educational assistance ($5,250)
• Qualified transportation ($315/month)
• Employee discounts
• De minimis fringe
flashcards.example: Term: $1M coverage, 20-year term, $50/month. Whole life: $500,000 coverage, $400/month, builds cash value.

flashcards.front: What is the NETTING process for capital gains and losses?
flashcards.back:
Capital Gain/Loss Netting:

1. Net ST gains vs ST losses
2. Net LT gains vs LT losses
3. Net ST vs LT

Results:
• Net ST gain: Ordinary rates
• Net LT gain: Preferential rates
• Net loss: Deduct $3,000, carry forward

Use losses against highest-taxed gains first

flashcards.front: Stock Basis Calculation
flashcards.back:
Purchase price
Add: Commissions paid
Improved/reinvested dividends
DRIP adjustments

Basis = What you paid (cost method)

Average cost allowed for mutual funds
Specific ID for tax loss harvesting

flashcards.front: State and Local Tax Deduction Limit
flashcards.back:
SALT cap (TCJA 2017):
• $10,000 limit ($5,000 MFS)
• State income OR sales tax
• Property taxes
• Combined limit

Sunsets after 2025 (reverts to unlimited)

Workaround: PTET (pass-through entity tax)

flashcards.front: Net Investment Income Tax
flashcards.back:
NIIT: 3.8% surtax on lesser of:
• Net investment income, OR
• MAGI over threshold

Thresholds:
• $250,000 MFJ
• $200,000 Single
• $125,000 MFS

NII = Interest, dividends, capital gains, rents, royalties, passive income (not wages)

flashcards.front: Common AMT Preference Items
flashcards.back:
Add back for AMT:
• State/local taxes (SALT)
• Miscellaneous itemized deductions
• Home equity interest (if not used for home)
• ISO bargain element (at exercise)
• Accelerated depreciation

Exemptions phase out at high income

flashcards.front: 2026 estate and gift tax exemption amounts?
flashcards.back:
Lifetime Estate/Gift Exemption: $7.0 million per person (post-TCJA sunset)

Annual Gift Exclusion: $19,000 per recipient

Married couples: $14.0 million combined (with portability)

Top estate/gift tax rate: 40%

Anti-clawback rule protects pre-2026 gifts made under higher exemption
flashcards.example: Annual exclusion $18,000 (2024). Parent gifts $50,000: $32,000 uses lifetime exemption or triggers gift tax.

flashcards.front: What is a REVOCABLE LIVING TRUST?
flashcards.back:
• Grantor can modify or revoke
• Avoids PROBATE (not estate tax)
• Assets remain in grantor"s estate
• Grantor is trustee while alive
• No income tax savings
• Provides privacy and disability planning
flashcards.example: 2024 exemption: $13.61M per person. Estate of $15M: taxable amount $1.39M × 40% = $556,000 federal estate tax.

flashcards.front: What is PORTABILITY of the estate tax exemption?
flashcards.back: Surviving spouse can use deceased spouse
flashcards.example: 2024 exemption: $13.61M per person. Estate of $15M: taxable amount $1.39M × 40% = $556,000 federal estate tax.

flashcards.front: Step-up in basis at death?
flashcards.back:
Inherited property receives NEW basis = FMV at date of death

Benefits:
• All unrealized gains erased
• Heirs can sell immediately with little/no tax

Exceptions:
• IRD assets (retirement accounts)
• Gifted property (carryover basis)
• Community property: BOTH halves step up
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What are valuation DISCOUNTS for closely-held businesses?
flashcards.back:
MINORITY DISCOUNT:
• Lack of control = 15-40% reduction

MARKETABILITY DISCOUNT:
• Cannot easily sell = 15-35% reduction

Combined discounts can reduce value 30-50%+

Used for gift/estate tax savings on family transfers
flashcards.example: 2024 exemption: $13.61M per person. Estate of $15M: taxable amount $1.39M × 40% = $556,000 federal estate tax.

flashcards.front: What is the per stirpes vs per capita distribution?
flashcards.back: PER STIRPES ("by branch"):\n• Deceased beneficiary

flashcards.front: What is the Rule of 72?
flashcards.back: Years to double = 72 ÷ Interest Rate\n\nExample: At 8%, money doubles in 9 years (72÷8)

flashcards.front: What is the difference between an ORDINARY ANNUITY and an ANNUITY DUE?
flashcards.back:
ORDINARY ANNUITY: Payments at END of period (most common)

ANNUITY DUE: Payments at BEGINNING of period

Annuity Due PV = Ordinary Annuity PV × (1+r)

flashcards.front: What are the 7 steps of the CFP financial planning process?
flashcards.back:
1. Understanding client circumstances
2. Identifying and selecting goals
3. Analyzing current course & alternatives
4. Developing recommendations
5. Presenting recommendations
6. Implementing recommendations
7. Monitoring progress and updating

flashcards.front: What is the recommended EMERGENCY FUND ratio?
flashcards.back:
Liquid Assets ÷ Monthly Expenses

• Stable income: 3-6 months
• Variable income: 6-12 months
• Approaching retirement: 12+ months
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What are the HOUSING (front-end) and TOTAL (back-end) debt ratio guidelines?
flashcards.back:
HOUSING RATIO: PITI ÷ Gross Income ≤ 28%

TOTAL DEBT RATIO: All Debt ÷ Gross Income ≤ 36%

FHA allows 31%/43%

flashcards.front: What is the difference between FISCAL and MONETARY policy?
flashcards.back: FISCAL POLICY: Government taxing and spending (Congress/President)\n\nMONETARY POLICY: Money supply and interest rates (Federal Reserve)

flashcards.front: 529 Plan contribution limits and gift tax treatment?
flashcards.back:
• No federal limit (state limits vary $300K-$500K+)
• Contributions = completed gifts (gift tax exclusion $19K/2026)
• 5-year superfunding: Up to $95K at once (5 × $19K)
• Tax-free growth and qualified withdrawals
flashcards.example: Annual exclusion $18,000 (2024). Parent gifts $50,000: $32,000 uses lifetime exemption or triggers gift tax.

flashcards.front: How are 529 plans treated for FAFSA?
flashcards.back:
PARENT-OWNED 529:
• Reported as parent asset
• Assessed at 5.64% maximum

GRANDPARENT-OWNED 529:
• NOT reported as asset (FAFSA Simplification Act)
• Distributions no longer count as student income
flashcards.example: Expected Family Contribution based on parent income, assets. Student assets count 20%, parent assets 5.64%.

flashcards.front: When should you ACCEPT vs REJECT an investment based on NPV?
flashcards.back:
NPV > 0: ACCEPT (adds value)
NPV < 0: REJECT (destroys value)
NPV = 0: Indifferent (earns exactly required return)

When comparing mutually exclusive projects, choose highest NPV.

flashcards.front: What is IRR and how do you use it?
flashcards.back:
IRR = Internal Rate of Return
The discount rate that makes NPV = 0

Decision Rule:
• If IRR > Required Return: Accept
• If IRR < Required Return: Reject

flashcards.front: What does BETA measure?
flashcards.back:
BETA = Systematic (market) risk

• β = 1: Moves with market
• β > 1: More volatile than market
• β < 1: Less volatile than market
• β < 0: Moves opposite to market

Beta only measures market risk, not total risk
flashcards.example: Stock with beta 1.2: if market rises 10%, expect stock to rise 12%. More volatile than market.

flashcards.front: Sharpe Ratio formula and interpretation?
flashcards.back:
Sharpe = (Rp - Rf) / σp

• Rp = Portfolio return
• Rf = Risk-free rate
• σp = Portfolio standard deviation

Interpretation:
• Higher = better risk-adjusted return
• > 1.0 is generally considered good
• Uses TOTAL risk (standard deviation)
flashcards.example: Fund A: 10% return, 15% std dev. Fund B: 10% return, 8% std dev. Fund B offers same return with less volatility (risk).

flashcards.front: CAPM formula for expected return?
flashcards.back:
E(R) = Rf + β(Rm - Rf)

• E(R) = Expected return
• Rf = Risk-free rate
• β = Beta
• Rm = Expected market return
• (Rm - Rf) = Market risk premium

Example: 3% + 1.2(10% - 3%) = 11.4%
flashcards.example: Stock with beta 1.2: if market rises 10%, expect stock to rise 12%. More volatile than market.

flashcards.front: What is DURATION and how is it used?
flashcards.back:
Duration = Weighted average time to receive cash flows

Measures interest rate sensitivity:
• If rates rise 1%, bond price falls approximately by duration %

Longer duration = MORE interest rate risk
Higher coupon = LOWER duration
Zero-coupon bond duration = maturity

flashcards.front: Bond price and yield relationship?
flashcards.back:
INVERSE relationship:
• When interest rates ↑, bond prices ↓
• When interest rates ↓, bond prices ↑

Long-term bonds: MORE price sensitive
Short-term bonds: LESS price sensitive

Premium bond: Coupon > YTM
Discount bond: Coupon < YTM

flashcards.front: What is the difference between STRATEGIC and TACTICAL asset allocation?
flashcards.back:
STRATEGIC:
• Long-term target allocation
• Based on goals, risk tolerance, time horizon
• Rebalance periodically to targets

TACTICAL:
• Short-term deviations from strategic
• Attempts to exploit market conditions
• More active management
• Higher costs/tax impact
flashcards.example: Moderate risk tolerance: 60% stocks (domestic/international), 35% bonds, 5% cash. Rebalance quarterly.

flashcards.front: Key differences between ETFs and mutual funds?
flashcards.back:
ETFs:
• Trade intraday like stocks
• Generally more tax-efficient
• Lower expense ratios typically
• No minimum investment
• In-kind redemptions reduce gains

Mutual Funds:
• Trade once daily at NAV
• May have minimums
• Capital gain distributions common
• Easier automatic investing
flashcards.example: Index fund: 0.05% ER. Active fund: 1.2% ER. On $100,000, difference is $1,150/year in fees.

flashcards.front: What are the duties under CFP Board\
flashcards.back:
Duty of LOYALTY: Client interests first
Duty of CARE: Competence, prudence, diligence
Duty to Follow Client INSTRUCTIONS

Applies at ALL TIMES when providing Financial Advice

Material conflicts must be disclosed, managed, and avoided when possible

flashcards.front: What is DISCLOSURE required under CFP Board Standards?
flashcards.back:
Must disclose IN WRITING:
• Material conflicts of interest
• All sources of compensation
• How conflicts are managed
• Scope of engagement
• Fees and costs
• Investment philosophy
• Broker-dealer/RIA affiliation

Disclosure must be updated as circumstances change

flashcards.front: What is the difference between RIA and Broker-Dealer regulation?
flashcards.back:
REGISTERED INVESTMENT ADVISER (RIA):
• SEC or State registered
• Fiduciary standard
• Form ADV disclosure
• Fee-based compensation

BROKER-DEALER:
• FINRA member
• Regulation Best Interest (Reg BI)
• Transaction-based compensation
• Form CRS disclosure
flashcards.example: CFP professional managing $500,000 must recommend lowest-cost index funds if appropriate, not high-commission products.

flashcards.front: When must a CFP® provide WRITTEN financial planning?
flashcards.back:
When providing FINANCIAL PLANNING (vs. Financial Advice):

• Must follow all 7 steps
• Recommendations must be in writing
• Must document the plan

Financial Advice alone doesn"t require written plan but still requires fiduciary duty
flashcards.example: CFP professional managing $500,000 must recommend lowest-cost index funds if appropriate, not high-commission products.

flashcards.front: When can a CFP® disclose client information WITHOUT consent?
flashcards.back:
Exceptions to confidentiality:

• Required by law (subpoena, court order)
• Defend against allegations by client
• Protect against suspected elder/financial abuse
• Report to appropriate authorities

CFP Board investigations require cooperation

flashcards.front: CFP® Continuing Education requirements?
flashcards.back:
Every 2-year reporting period:

• 30 hours total CE
• Minimum 2 hours CFP Board Ethics CE
• Remaining hours in CFP Board-approved programs

Must also:
• Complete annual certification
• Report any matters (bankruptcy, complaints, etc.)

flashcards.front: What client information is needed for suitability/best interest?
flashcards.back:
At minimum, understand:

• Investment objectives
• Time horizon
• Risk tolerance
• Financial situation
• Tax status
• Liquidity needs
• Other investments
• Experience/knowledge

Without this, cannot make suitable recommendation
flashcards.example: Questionnaire scores: time horizon 10+ years (high), loss tolerance 15% (moderate), income stability high → moderate-aggressive allocation.

flashcards.front: Types of financial planner compensation?
flashcards.back:
FEE-ONLY:
• Fees paid directly by client only
• No commissions or third-party payments

FEE-BASED:
• Fees and commissions
• Creates conflicts requiring disclosure

COMMISSION-ONLY:
• Paid by product sales
• Most conflicts of interest

flashcards.front: 2026 401(k) contribution limits?
flashcards.back:
Employee Elective Deferral: $24,500
Catch-up (age 50+): +$7,500 = $32,000 total
Super catch-up (60-63): +$11,250

Total Annual Addition (employer + employee): $71,500
With catch-up: $79,000

flashcards.front: 2026 IRA contribution limits and income phase-outs?
flashcards.back:
Contribution: $7,500 ($8,500 with catch-up 50+)

Traditional IRA Deduction Phase-out (w/employer plan):
• Single: $81,000-$91,000
• MFJ: $129,000-$149,000

Roth IRA Phase-out:
• Single: $154,000-$169,000
• MFJ: $240,000-$250,000
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: Social Security EARLY claiming reduction vs DELAYED credits?
flashcards.back:
EARLY (before FRA):
• 5/9 of 1% per month for first 36 months = 20%
• 5/12 of 1% per additional month = ~6.67%/year
• At 62 (FRA 67): 30% reduction

DELAYED (after FRA):
• 8% per year increase until age 70
• Maximum: 24% increase at 70
flashcards.example: Full retirement age 67, monthly benefit $2,500. Claiming at 62: $2,500 × 0.7 = $1,750/month (30% reduction).

flashcards.front: When do RMDs begin for retirement accounts?
flashcards.back: Traditional IRA/401(k): Age 73 (born 1951-1959), Age 75 (born 1960+)\n\nRoth IRA: NO RMDs during owner
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: What is a 72(t) SEPP and how does it work?
flashcards.back:
Substantially Equal Periodic Payments

• Avoids 10% early withdrawal penalty before 59½
• Must continue for 5 years OR until 59½ (whichever is LATER)
• Three methods: RMD, Amortization, Annuitization
• Modification triggers penalty on ALL prior distributions

flashcards.front: What is the 4% SAFE WITHDRAWAL RATE rule?
flashcards.back:
Withdraw 4% of portfolio in year 1, adjust for inflation thereafter

Based on 30-year retirement with 50/50 portfolio

Formula: Annual Need ÷ 0.04 = Required Portfolio

Example: $60,000/year needs $1.5 million
flashcards.example: Client needs $80,000/year for 25-year retirement. At 4% withdrawal rate, requires $2,000,000 portfolio at retirement.

flashcards.front: SEP-IRA vs SIMPLE IRA comparison?
flashcards.back:
SEP-IRA:
• Employer only contributions (up to 25% of comp or $71.5K)
• No catch-up
• Deadline: Tax return due date

SIMPLE IRA:
• Employee: $17,000 ($20,500 catch-up)
• Employer: 2% non-elective OR 3% match
• 2-year rule for rollovers
• <100 employees
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: What is a BACKDOOR ROTH IRA strategy?
flashcards.back:
1. Contribute to non-deductible Traditional IRA
2. Convert immediately to Roth IRA
3. Pay tax only on gains (if any)

Caveat: Pro-rata rule applies if you have existing Traditional IRA balances

Used when income exceeds Roth contribution limits
flashcards.example: Traditional: $7,000 contribution (2024), tax-deductible if income eligible. Roth: after-tax, tax-free withdrawals.

flashcards.front: What are the two IRS-required vesting schedules?
flashcards.back:
CLIFF VESTING:
• 0% until 3 years, then 100%

GRADED VESTING:
• Year 2: 20%
• Year 3: 40%
• Year 4: 60%
• Year 5: 80%
• Year 6: 100%

Employee contributions always 100% vested immediately

flashcards.front: What are the advantages of a Solo 401(k)?
flashcards.back:
• Higher contribution limits ($71.5K + catch-up)
• Employee + Employer contributions
• Roth option available
• Loan provision allowed
• No plan discrimination testing
• Self-employed and spouse only
• Can do mega backdoor Roth

flashcards.front: Types of permanent life insurance?
flashcards.back:
WHOLE LIFE: Fixed premiums, guaranteed death benefit, cash value

UNIVERSAL LIFE: Flexible premiums, adjustable death benefit

VARIABLE LIFE: Investment subaccounts, market risk to policyholder

VARIABLE UNIVERSAL LIFE: Combines UL flexibility with VL investment options
flashcards.example: Term: $1M coverage, 20-year term, $50/month. Whole life: $500,000 coverage, $400/month, builds cash value.

flashcards.front: Own-occupation vs Any-occupation disability definition?
flashcards.back: OWN OCCUPATION (best):\n• Disabled if can

flashcards.front: Long-term care insurance benefit triggers (ADLs)?
flashcards.back:
Benefits triggered when unable to perform 2 of 6 ADLs:

1. Bathing
2. Dressing
3. Eating
4. Toileting
5. Transferring (moving in/out of bed)
6. Continence

OR cognitive impairment
flashcards.example: Policy: $200/day benefit, 3-year benefit period, 90-day elimination. Lifetime benefit: $219,000.

flashcards.front: What do HO-3 and HO-5 homeowners policies cover?
flashcards.back:
HO-3 (Special Form):
• Dwelling: Open perils (all risks)
• Contents: Named perils only
• Most common policy

HO-5 (Comprehensive):
• Dwelling: Open perils
• Contents: Open perils
• Most coverage, higher premium

flashcards.front: What does 100/300/50 auto liability mean?
flashcards.back:
$100,000 per person bodily injury
$300,000 per accident bodily injury
$50,000 property damage per accident

This is often minimum for umbrella policy eligibility

flashcards.front: What is a 1035 exchange?
flashcards.back:
Tax-free exchange of insurance/annuity contracts:

• Life → Life ✓
• Life → Annuity ✓
• Annuity → Annuity ✓
• Annuity → Life ✗

Cost basis carries over
Must be direct transfer between insurers

flashcards.front: Four risk management techniques?
flashcards.back: AVOID: Don

flashcards.front: What is the Human Life Value approach?
flashcards.back:
Calculate PV of future earnings to determine life insurance need

Steps:
1. Estimate annual earnings
2. Subtract taxes and personal consumption
3. Determine years until retirement
4. Discount to present value

Simple estimate: 20-25× annual income
flashcards.example: To have $50,000 in 10 years at 5% return: PV = $50,000 / (1.05)^10 = $30,696 needed today.

flashcards.front: What does an umbrella liability policy cover?
flashcards.back:
Excess liability coverage above underlying policies

Covers:
• Bodily injury/property damage
• Personal injury (libel, slander)
• Drops down if underlying excludes

Typical limits: $1M-$5M+
Cost: ~$200-$400/year for $1M

Requires adequate underlying limits

flashcards.front: 2026 Standard Deduction amounts?
flashcards.back:
Single: $15,350
MFJ: $30,700
MFS: $15,350
HOH: $22,900

Additional for 65+ or blind:
• Single/HOH: +$2,050
• MFJ/MFS: +$1,650 each

flashcards.front: Long-term capital gains tax rates?
flashcards.back:
0% Rate:
• Single: Up to $47,025
• MFJ: Up to $94,050

15% Rate: Middle income

20% Rate:
• Single: Over $518,900
• MFJ: Over $583,750

Plus 3.8% NIIT above $200K/$250K

flashcards.front: What triggers the Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT)?
flashcards.back:
Common AMT triggers:
• Large state/local tax deductions (SALT)
• Exercising Incentive Stock Options (ISOs)
• Private activity bond interest
• Accelerated depreciation
• Large miscellaneous deductions

2024 exemptions: $85,700 Single / $133,300 MFJ

flashcards.front: Child Tax Credit vs Dependent Care Credit?
flashcards.back:
CHILD TAX CREDIT:
• $2,000 per child under 17
• $1,700 refundable
• Phase-out: $200K/$400K

DEPENDENT CARE CREDIT:
• Up to $3,000 (1 child) or $6,000 (2+)
• Credit = 20-35% of expenses
• Non-refundable

flashcards.front: Charitable deduction limits for cash vs appreciated property?
flashcards.back:
CASH: Up to 60% of AGI

APPRECIATED PROPERTY (LTCG):
• Public charity: 30% of AGI
• Private foundation: 20% of AGI

5-year carryforward for excess

No gain recognized on appreciated property donation

flashcards.front: What is the Kiddie Tax and who does it apply to?
flashcards.back:
Applies to children:
• Under 19 (or under 24 if student)
• With unearned income > $2,500 (2024)

First $1,250: Tax-free
Next $1,250: Child

flashcards.front: What is the Qualified Business Income (QBI) deduction?
flashcards.back:
Section 199A: Deduct up to 20% of QBI

Limitations for high income:
• SSTB phase-out: $191,950-$241,950 (single)
• W-2 wage/capital limit

Not available for:
• C-Corporations
• Employee wages
• Investment income

flashcards.front: Key requirements for a 1031 like-kind exchange?
flashcards.back:
• Like-kind property (real estate for real estate)
• Investment or business use (not personal)
• 45-day identification period
• 180-day closing period
• Boot (non-like-kind) is taxable
• Qualified intermediary required

Defers capital gains indefinitely

flashcards.front: What is the 3.8% Net Investment Income Tax (NIIT)?
flashcards.back:
Additional 3.8% tax on LESSER OF:
• Net investment income, OR
• MAGI over threshold

Thresholds:
• Single: $200,000
• MFJ: $250,000

Investment income includes:
• Dividends, interest, capital gains
• Rental/royalty income
• Passive business income
